metadata.title: How to Grow an LSM-tree? Towards Bridging the Gap Between Theory and Practice
metadata.titleTranslation: 如何让 LSM-tree 增长？迈向弥合理论与实践之间的鸿沟
metadata.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data, Volume 3, Number 3 (SIGMOD), Article 173

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown:
LSM-tree based key-value stores are widely adopted as the data storage backend in modern big data applications. The LSM-tree grows with data ingestion, by either adding levels with fixed level capacities (dubbed as vertical scheme) or increasing level capacities with fixed number of levels (dubbed as horizontal scheme). The vertical scheme leads the trend in recent system designs in RocksDB, LevelDB, and WiredTiger, whereas the horizontal scheme shows a decline in being adopted in the industry. The growth scheme profoundly impacts the LSM system performance in various aspects such as read, write and space costs. This paper attempts to give a new insight into a fundamental design question – how to grow an LSM-tree to attain more desirable performance?

Our analysis highlights the limitations of the vertical scheme in achieving an optimal read-write trade-off and the horizontal scheme in managing space cost effectively. Building on the analysis, we present a novel approach, Vertiorizon, which combines the strengths of both the vertical and horizontal schemes to achieve a superior balance between lookup, update, and space costs. Its adaptive design makes it highly compatible with a wide spectrum of workloads. Compared to the vertical scheme, Vertiorizon significantly improves the read-write performance trade-off. In contrast to the horizontal scheme, Vertiorizon greatly extends the trade-off range by a non-trivial generalization of Bentley and Saxe’s theory [7], while substantially reducing space costs. When integrated with RocksDB, Vertiorizon demonstrates better write performance than the vertical scheme, while incurring about six times less additional space cost compared to the horizontal scheme.
sections.translationMarkdown:
基于 LSM-tree 的 key-value store 被广泛用作现代大数据应用的数据存储后端。随着数据摄入，LSM-tree 通过两种方式之一增长：增加具有固定层容量的层（称为垂直方案），或在层数固定的情况下增加各层容量（称为水平方案）。在 RocksDB、LevelDB 和 WiredTiger 等近期系统设计中，垂直方案已成为主流，而水平方案在工业界的采用率则呈下降趋势。增长方案会从读、写和空间成本等多个方面深刻影响 LSM 系统的性能。本文试图为一个基础设计问题提供新的认识——应当如何增长 LSM-tree，才能获得更理想的性能？

我们的分析揭示了垂直方案在实现最优读写权衡方面的局限，以及水平方案在有效管理空间成本方面的不足。在此分析基础上，我们提出了一种新方法 Vertiorizon，它结合垂直方案与水平方案的优势，在 lookup、update 和空间成本之间实现更优的平衡。其自适应设计使其能够高度兼容广泛的 workload。与垂直方案相比，Vertiorizon 显著改善了读写性能权衡。与水平方案相比，Vertiorizon 通过对 Bentley 和 Saxe 理论 [7] 的非平凡推广大幅扩展了权衡范围，同时显著降低空间成本。集成到 RocksDB 后，Vertiorizon 展现出优于垂直方案的写性能；与水平方案相比，其额外空间成本约低六倍。

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.originalMarkdown:
LSM-tree is a key-value data structure that organizes data into multiple increasing levels. It appends incoming data entries into the buffer, whose filled-up triggers a merge-sort of the buffer into the next level. The process can cascade down whenever the affected level reaches its capacity to ensure desired read performance and minimize the space amplification. Due to its desired performance, the LSM-tree is extensively employed as the backbone of various data storage systems. This includes its utilization in key-value stores (KV-stores) like RocksDB [20], Cassandra [34], and ScyllaDB [53], NewSQL stores such as CockroachDB [33], as well as time-series databases like InfluxDB [30].

*Fig. 1. Different growth schemes of LSM-trees.*

**Vertical and Horizontal Growth Schemes.** The growth scheme governs how LSM-trees expand and maintain their hierarchical structure amidst the continuous influx of new data entries. Specifically, the LSM-tree relies on compactions to transfer data from one level to the next, ensuring that each level’s size is significantly larger than the preceding one. The growth scheme fundamentally controls the timing of these compactions at each level. Currently, the growth schemes adopted in mainstream LSM-based key-value stores fall into two major categories, the vertical scheme and the horizontal scheme. With the vertical scheme, data ingestion into the LSM-tree would expand it vertically by creating additional levels. Specifically, each LSM-tree level has a fixed capacity that increases exponentially with the number of levels, as Figure 1 (a) depicts. Compactions are initiated from one level to the subsequent level as soon as the level is full. Under the horizontal scheme, the total number of levels on the disk is confined to a fixed value. As illustrated in Figure 1 (b), each level grows horizontally with a heuristic (see Section 3) that generally maintains the relative capacity between levels, implying that their capacities would gradually expand as data flows in. Beyond the last level, each level initiates a compaction into the subsequent level after a certain number of compactions from its preceding level. The required number of compactions gradually increases as the levels grow.

| Growth Scheme | Vertical | Horizontal |
|---|---|---|
| Leveling Merge | LevelDB, RocksDB, WiredTiger, BadgerDB ... | BigTable, HBase, AsterixDB |
| Tiering Merge | Cassandra, ScyllaDB | Not exist |

*Table 1. The landscape of the two growth schemes.*

**The Decline of Horizontal Scheme V.S. Its Desired Theoretical Property.** The horizontal scheme had been adopted by many pioneering key-value storage systems, including Bigtable [10], HBase [23], and AsterixDB [4]. However, a noteworthy trend in recent ten years is that most of the new generation key-value storage systems employ the vertical scheme, such as LevelDB [25], RocksDB [20], WiredTiger [13], Cassandra [34], ScyllaDB [53], and BadgerDB [12], as Table 1 summarizes. Furthermore, a majority of the prominent NoSQL databases in recent times, such as CockroachDB [33], TiDB [47], Dgraph [19], and PolarDB [26], have opted for the vertical scheme in their storage backend. This is probably attributed to that the vertical scheme offers the advantage of being easier to manage, and it allows for a finer compaction granularity to mitigate space amplification and write stalls. Contrary to this trend, interestingly, an early theory back in 1980’s [7] revealed that the horizontal-growth-style data structure could offer an optimal trade-off between read and write costs even before the formal invention of the LSM-tree, and this theory is further explicitly discussed in the context of LSM-trees by Mathieu et al. [45]. This controversy strongly indicates that the horizontal growth scheme might be undervalued in practical system design choices.

| Designs | Vertical-Leveling | Vertical-Tiering | Horizontal-Leveling | Horizontal-Tiering (ours) | Vertiorizon (ours) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Update-Heavy | ⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ |
| Lookup-heavy | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ |
| Space | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ |

*Table 2. Comparing existing growth schemes and ours.*

**The Problem: The Imperfect LSM-tree Growth Scheme Hurts System Performance.** How to grow (a.k.a., expand) the LSM-trees with fast data writes is among the most critical design choices, profoundly impacting the performance of LSM-tree based systems. For instance, in a cloud environment using LSM-based storage, the chosen growth scheme impacts the system latency, write amplification, and space cost during data ingestion. A suboptimal growth scheme leads to higher write amplification, requiring more disk scans, which shortens hardware lifespan and raises cloud costs. Additionally, ineffective growth schemes result in increased latency and space usage, driving up costs for users storing and querying data. This is because the growth scheme dictates the timing of compactions, and inappropriate compaction timing can lead to higher write and read amplification (as we detail later in Sections 3 and 4), thereby increasing latency. Furthermore, different schemes support varying compaction granularities, while full compaction in particular may cause high space amplification, resulting in increased storage cost. Existing growth schemes thus face limitations that hinder system scalability in various ways. The vertical scheme, used by RocksDB and LevelDB, is not optimal in balancing read-write trade-offs because it performs compaction at each level with a fixed frequency, leading to reduced system throughput as data scales. The horizontal scheme, employed by BigTable, has only been implemented with the leveling merge policy, making it difficult to handle update-intensive workloads—a common workload type in modern systems. It also requires full compaction during merges, resulting in high space overhead. This motivates us to revisit the strengths and weaknesses of the two growth schemes, and ask for the possibility of better designs.

**Key Intuition 1.** There is an increasing need for a systematic discussion on the foundational topic of LSM-tree growth schemes, due to the growing scale and complexity of LSM-based systems. One important recent design is to adopt the tiering merge policy (i.e., each level is compacted as a new sorted run in the subsequent level), rather than the default leveling merge policy (i.e., each level is compacted to the existing sorted run in the subsequent level), to improve write performance at the expense of read performance. As demonstrated by Dayan and Idreos [15], the vertical growth scheme can be easily combined with the tiering policy, increasing its flexibility in balancing read-write trade-offs. However, the integration of the horizontal scheme with the tiering merge policy remains unexplored, and no such design has been adopted previously (Table 1). As a result, the horizontal scheme offers limited flexibility in balancing read and write performance, which is particularly undesirable for workloads with frequent updates. Our first intuition is, what if we can design a horizontal-tiering scheme that couples horizontal scheme with tiering merge policy? Such a design, if feasible, could significantly elevate the status of the horizontal scheme and revive interest in this largely overlooked approach. However, creating an effective horizontal-tiering scheme is notoriously difficult and has not been accomplished since the invention of LSM-trees.

**Key Intuition 2.** Since the strengths and weaknesses of the two major growth schemes complement each other, our second intuition is whether it is possible to combine the two to achieve the best of both worlds. There are countless potential ways to hybridize these schemes, but ensuring the combination fully leverages the strengths of both is challenging. Moreover, the optimal growth scheme often depends on the specific workload. For example, a read-intensive workload might favor a leveling-based scheme over a tiering-based one. This implies the necessity of a growth scheme that can be adaptive to different workloads, adding to the sophistication in hybridizing the schemes.

**Our Proposal.** We present two novel techniques, namely Horizontal-Tiering and Vertiorizon, to address the challenges. Horizontal-tiering enhances the horizontal scheme with the integration of the tiering policy (response to Intuition 1). This technique will revoke the recognition of the largely overlooked horizontal scheme, and thus highly impact the landscape of LSM-tree growth schemes. On top of this enhanced scheme, we further propose a new design Vertiorizon, which is a novel self-designing LSM-tree growth scheme that harnesses the strengths of both vertical and horizontal schemes, obtaining both theoretical merits and desired practical performance (response to Intuition 2). As shown in Figure 10 (a), Horizontal-Tiering effectively expands the optimal read-write trade-off to a much wider spectrum. As shown in Table 2, compared to existing schemes like Vertical-Leveling, Vertical-Tiering, and Horizontal-Leveling, Vertiorizon offers very robust performance across all dimensions. We summarize our contributions as follows.

- We carefully review the pros and cons of the two major growth schemes for LSM-trees, and motivates the fundamental problem in designing better growth schemes.
- We present the horizontal-tiering scheme, which is the first horizontal scheme that can be effectively applied under the tiering merge policy since the invention of LSM-trees. We also theoretically show that our designed horizontal-tiering scheme achieves the minimum accumulative cost during compaction. This is a non-trivial generalization of Bentley and Saxe’s theory [7].
- We present Vertiorizon, an innovative growth scheme that embodies the strengths of both the vertical and horizontal schemes, which also offers a nice self-designing feature to work in an adaptive manner with different workloads.
- We design a novel algorithm to adapt our design to work under more skewed workloads. This is accompanied with a deep analysis to justify the rationale behind such designs.
- We present typically how Vertiorizon can be embedded in a mature LSM-system by introducing a novel dynamic Bloom filter layout tailored to our design.
- We carry out extensive experimental assessments of Vertiorizon in real-world system environments. We demonstrate that Vertiorizon achieves up to 3.2 times throughput than the vertical scheme (employed by RocksDB), and incurs about 6 times less additional space amplification compared to the horizontal scheme.
sections.translationMarkdown:
LSM-tree 是一种将数据组织到多个逐渐增大的层中的 key-value 数据结构。它将新到达的数据项追加到 buffer 中；buffer 填满后，会触发一次 merge-sort，将 buffer 中的数据并入下一层。只要受影响的层达到其容量，这一过程就可能向下级联，以保证所需的读性能并尽量减小空间放大。由于性能理想，LSM-tree 被广泛用作各种数据存储系统的骨干结构，包括 RocksDB [20]、Cassandra [34] 和 ScyllaDB [53] 等 key-value store（KV-store），CockroachDB [33] 等 NewSQL store，以及 InfluxDB [30] 等时序数据库。

*图 1. LSM-tree 的不同增长方案。*

**垂直与水平增长方案。** 在新数据项持续涌入的过程中，增长方案决定 LSM-tree 如何扩展并维持其层次结构。具体而言，LSM-tree 依靠 compaction 将数据从一层转移到下一层，并确保每一层的大小显著大于前一层。增长方案从根本上控制各层 compaction 的时机。目前，主流基于 LSM 的 key-value store 所采用的增长方案可分为两大类：垂直方案和水平方案。采用垂直方案时，数据摄入会通过创建更多层使 LSM-tree 沿垂直方向扩展。具体来说，如图 1(a) 所示，每个 LSM-tree 层都有固定容量，并且容量随层号呈指数增长。一旦某层被填满，就会立即发起从该层到下一层的 compaction。在水平方案下，磁盘上的总层数被限制为一个固定值。如图 1(b) 所示，每一层依据一种启发式方法（见第 3 节）沿水平方向增长；该方法通常维持各层之间的相对容量，这意味着随着数据流入，各层容量会逐步扩大。除最后一层外，每一层会在其前一层完成一定次数的 compaction 后，向下一层发起一次 compaction。随着各层增长，所要求的 compaction 次数也会逐渐增加。

| 增长方案 | 垂直 | 水平 |
|---|---|---|
| Leveling merge | LevelDB、RocksDB、WiredTiger、BadgerDB…… | BigTable、HBase、AsterixDB |
| Tiering merge | Cassandra、ScyllaDB | 不存在 |

*表 1. 两种增长方案的应用格局。*

**水平方案的衰落与其理想的理论性质。** 许多开创性的 key-value 存储系统曾采用水平方案，包括 Bigtable [10]、HBase [23] 和 AsterixDB [4]。然而，近十年来一个值得注意的趋势是，大多数新一代 key-value 存储系统都采用垂直方案，例如 LevelDB [25]、RocksDB [20]、WiredTiger [13]、Cassandra [34]、ScyllaDB [53] 和 BadgerDB [12]，如表 1 所总结。此外，近来大多数有代表性的 NoSQL 数据库，例如 CockroachDB [33]、TiDB [47]、Dgraph [19] 和 PolarDB [26]，其存储后端也选择了垂直方案。这可能是因为垂直方案具有更易管理的优点，并且它允许采用更细粒度的 compaction，从而缓解空间放大和写停顿。与这一趋势相反，颇为有趣的是，早在 20 世纪 80 年代的一项理论 [7] 就揭示：甚至在 LSM-tree 被正式发明之前，水平增长风格的数据结构便可以在读成本和写成本之间提供最优权衡；Mathieu 等人 [45] 后来又在 LSM-tree 语境下明确讨论了该理论。这一矛盾强烈表明，水平增长方案在实际系统的设计选择中可能被低估了。

| 设计 | Vertical-Leveling | Vertical-Tiering | Horizontal-Leveling | Horizontal-Tiering（本文） | Vertiorizon（本文） |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Update-heavy | ⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ |
| Lookup-heavy | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆⋆ |
| 空间 | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆ | ⋆⋆⋆⋆ |

*表 2. 现有增长方案与本文方案的比较。*

**问题：不完善的 LSM-tree 增长方案会损害系统性能。** 在高速写入数据时如何增长（亦即扩展）LSM-tree，是最关键的设计选择之一，它会深刻影响基于 LSM-tree 的系统性能。例如，在使用基于 LSM 的存储的云环境中，所选增长方案会影响数据摄入期间的系统延迟、写放大和空间成本。次优的增长方案会导致更高的写放大，需要更多磁盘扫描，从而缩短硬件寿命并提高云成本。此外，低效的增长方案还会增加延迟和空间使用量，提高用户存储和查询数据的成本。这是因为增长方案决定了 compaction 时机；不恰当的 compaction 时机会造成更高的写放大与读放大（我们将在第 3 节和第 4 节中详细说明），进而增加延迟。此外，不同方案支持不同的 compaction 粒度，而 full compaction 尤其可能造成较高的空间放大，导致存储成本上升。因此，现有增长方案存在从多个方面阻碍系统可扩展性的局限。RocksDB 和 LevelDB 所采用的垂直方案并不能最优地平衡读写权衡，因为它以固定频率在每一层执行 compaction，随着数据规模扩大，这会降低系统吞吐量。BigTable 所采用的水平方案则只在 leveling merge policy 下得到实现，因而难以处理 update-intensive workload——这是现代系统中常见的一类 workload。它在 merge 时还要求执行 full compaction，造成很高的空间开销。这促使我们重新审视两种增长方案的优缺点，并探究是否可能设计出更好的方案。

**关键直觉 1。** 随着基于 LSM 的系统规模与复杂性不断增长，越来越有必要系统地讨论 LSM-tree 增长方案这一基础主题。近期一项重要设计是采用 tiering merge policy（即将每一层 compact 为下一层中的一个新 sorted run），而不是默认的 leveling merge policy（即将每一层 compact 到下一层已有的 sorted run 中），从而以牺牲读性能为代价改善写性能。Dayan 和 Idreos [15] 已经证明，垂直增长方案很容易与 tiering policy 结合，提高其平衡读写权衡的灵活性。然而，水平方案与 tiering merge policy 的结合仍未得到探索，过去也没有采用这种组合的设计（表 1）。因此，水平方案平衡读写性能的灵活性有限，这对于频繁 update 的 workload 尤其不理想。我们的第一个直觉是：如果能设计一种将水平方案与 tiering merge policy 结合起来的 horizontal-tiering 方案，会怎样？如果可行，这种设计可能显著提升水平方案的地位，并重新唤起人们对这一长期被忽视的方法的兴趣。然而，设计有效的 horizontal-tiering 方案极其困难，自 LSM-tree 发明以来一直未能实现。

**关键直觉 2。** 由于两大增长方案的优缺点彼此互补，我们的第二个直觉是：能否将二者结合，从而兼得两者之长？混合这些方案的潜在方式不计其数，但要确保组合方式充分发挥双方优势并不容易。此外，最优增长方案往往取决于具体 workload。例如，与基于 tiering 的方案相比，读密集型 workload 可能更偏好基于 leveling 的方案。这意味着需要一种能够适应不同 workload 的增长方案，也进一步增加了混合这些方案的复杂性。

**我们的方案。** 我们提出 Horizontal-Tiering 和 Vertiorizon 两项新技术来应对这些挑战。Horizontal-tiering 通过集成 tiering policy 增强水平方案（回应直觉 1）。该技术将重新唤起人们对长期被忽视的水平方案的认识，并由此深刻影响 LSM-tree 增长方案的整体格局。在这一增强方案之上，我们进一步提出新设计 Vertiorizon：一种新颖的 self-designing LSM-tree 增长方案，它同时利用垂直与水平方案的优势，既具备理论上的优点，也获得理想的实际性能（回应直觉 2）。如图 10(a) 所示，Horizontal-Tiering 有效地将最优读写权衡扩展到宽得多的范围。如表 2 所示，与 Vertical-Leveling、Vertical-Tiering 和 Horizontal-Leveling 等现有方案相比，Vertiorizon 在所有维度上都提供了非常稳健的性能。我们的贡献总结如下。

- 我们仔细回顾了两种主要 LSM-tree 增长方案的优缺点，并由此引出设计更优增长方案这一基础问题。
- 我们提出 horizontal-tiering 方案，这是自 LSM-tree 发明以来，第一个能够有效应用于 tiering merge policy 的水平方案。我们还从理论上证明，所设计的 horizontal-tiering 方案在 compaction 期间实现了最小累积成本。这是对 Bentley 和 Saxe 理论 [7] 的非平凡推广。
- 我们提出 Vertiorizon，这是一种体现垂直与水平方案优势的创新增长方案，同时还具有良好的 self-designing 特性，能够以自适应方式应对不同 workload。
- 我们设计了一种新算法，使我们的设计能够适应更倾斜的 workload，并通过深入分析论证这些设计背后的原理。
- 我们通过引入专为本设计定制的新型动态 Bloom filter 布局，具体展示 Vertiorizon 如何嵌入成熟的 LSM 系统。
- 我们在真实系统环境中对 Vertiorizon 开展了广泛的实验评估。结果表明，Vertiorizon 的吞吐量最高可达到垂直方案（RocksDB 所采用方案）的 3.2 倍；与水平方案相比，其额外空间放大约低 6 倍。

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.originalMarkdown:
**LSM-tree.** The LSM-tree [46] is a persistent multi-level index structure designed to store key-value entries. An LSM-tree achieves efficient write performance by transforming random writes to the disk into sequential writes. Specifically, when a key-value entry is inserted, the LSM-tree first temporarily stores it in a main memory buffer. Once the buffer reaches its capacity, all entries within it are merged and sorted by keys into the first level on the disk. To gain better write performance, LSM-tree organizes data across various sorted levels on the disk, arranged in ascending order of size. When certain conditions are met, the LSM-tree would merge one level into the subsequent level through compaction, continuing in this manner up to the largest level. In this way, the write amplification associated with merging data from the buffer or a specific level to the subsequent one is duly mitigated. Write amplification quantifies the average count of physical rewrites for each entry, due to the recurrent data rewrites to the storage driven by the compaction process inherent to the LSM-tree. Read amplification measures the average number of disk pages read for a single lookup, as a search might scan through several sorted runs within an LSM-tree to find a specific key. Space amplification evaluates the additional space cost needed on average for each unique entry.

**Full and Partial Compactions.** Usually, a compaction merges and sorts the full data from one level with the next, known as full compaction. However, in LSM-trees, a deeper level could hold vast amounts of data, and full compaction in such cases can cause major performance issues. This not only slows down the system but also leads to substantial space amplification, as original data cannot be disposed of until compaction is fully done. The partial compaction mitigates these problems, allowing merging only a small fraction of the data with the subsequent level in each compaction.

**Tiering and Leveling Merge Policy.** Under the leveling merge policy, a compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$ merge-sorts all data from Level $i$ with all the existing data in Level $i + 1$. Additionally, each buffer flush merge-sorts its data with the existing data in the first level. In contrast, the tiering merge policy handles compactions or buffer flushes to a level by adding incoming data as a new separate sorted run in that level, without merging it with the existing data there. In this way, each entry is involved in only one compaction at each level. Generally, the leveling merge policy is more read-friendly, while tiering performs better in write-intensive scenarios.

**Point Lookup and Range Lookup.** Point-lookup locates the entry for a given key. During the lookup, the sorted run is probed from the newest to the oldest, until the entry containing the queried key is found. Modern LSM-based systems typically store the starting key of each disk block as an index in memory. Since each LSM-tree run is sorted, this setup allows for a direct pinpoint of the sole block likely to contain a particular key, ensuring a maximum of one I/O for each lookup at every sorted run. This process is further accelerated by applying Bloom filter at each sorted run. The filters will be loaded into the main memory when the system starts. To check whether a key exists in a run, the Bloom filter associated with the run is first probed. An I/O is incurred only when the filter returns true, otherwise the access to the sorted run can be exempted. In contrast, range-lookup aims to extract the entries for a given key range. The process is similar to point-lookup, except that (1) Bloom filter is not applied; and (2) every sorted run is accessed.

**Read-write Trade-off.** The “read-write trade-off” in an LSM-tree is the balance between lookup and update costs. By changing its structure, the LSM-tree can optimize for either lookups or updates, but improving one comes at the expense of the other. Different growth schemes produce different trade-off curves, showing how much one cost must be sacrificed to improve the other. A design with a better read-write trade-off requires less compromise in one cost to achieve a certain performance level in the other.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**LSM-tree。** LSM-tree [46] 是一种用于存储 key-value entry 的持久化多层索引结构。LSM-tree 将对磁盘的随机写转化为顺序写，从而实现高效的写性能。具体来说，插入一个 key-value entry 时，LSM-tree 首先将其暂存于主存 buffer。buffer 达到容量后，其中所有 entry 会按 key 进行 merge-sort，并写入磁盘上的第一层。为了获得更好的写性能，LSM-tree 将数据组织在磁盘上的多个有序层中，这些层按大小升序排列。当满足特定条件时，LSM-tree 会通过 compaction 将一层 merge 到下一层，并以这种方式一直进行到最大层。这样便能适当缓解将数据从 buffer 或某一层 merge 到下一层所带来的写放大。写放大量化每个 entry 平均发生的物理重写次数；这些重写源于 LSM-tree 固有的 compaction 过程反复将数据写回存储设备。读放大量化单次 lookup 平均读取的磁盘页数，因为一次搜索可能需要扫描 LSM-tree 中多个 sorted run 才能找到特定 key。空间放大量化每个唯一 entry 平均所需的额外空间成本。

**Full compaction 与 partial compaction。** 通常，一次 compaction 会将某一层的全部数据与下一层 merge 并排序，这称为 full compaction。然而在 LSM-tree 中，较深的层可能保存海量数据，此时 full compaction 会造成严重的性能问题。它不仅会降低系统速度，还会导致显著的空间放大，因为 compaction 完全结束之前不能删除原始数据。Partial compaction 可以缓解这些问题，它允许每次 compaction 只将一小部分数据 merge 到下一层。

**Tiering 与 Leveling Merge Policy。** 在 leveling merge policy 下，从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的一次 compaction，会将 Level $i$ 的全部数据与 Level $i + 1$ 中已有的全部数据进行 merge-sort。此外，每次 buffer flush 都会将其数据与第一层已有数据进行 merge-sort。相比之下，在 tiering merge policy 下，向某一层进行 compaction 或 buffer flush 时，会把新到达的数据作为该层中一个独立的新 sorted run 加入，而不与该层已有数据 merge。这样，每个 entry 在每一层只参与一次 compaction。总体而言，leveling merge policy 对读更友好，而 tiering 在写密集型场景下表现更好。

**Point Lookup 与 Range Lookup。** Point-lookup 用于定位给定 key 对应的 entry。在 lookup 过程中，会按照从新到旧的顺序探测 sorted run，直到找到包含所查询 key 的 entry。现代基于 LSM 的系统通常会将每个磁盘块的起始 key 作为索引存储在内存中。由于每个 LSM-tree run 都是有序的，这种设置可以直接定位唯一可能包含特定 key 的块，确保在每个 sorted run 上，每次 lookup 最多产生一次 I/O。对每个 sorted run 应用 Bloom filter 可以进一步加速这一过程。系统启动时，这些 filter 会被载入主存。为了检查某个 key 是否存在于一个 run 中，首先探测与该 run 关联的 Bloom filter。只有 filter 返回 true 时才会产生一次 I/O；否则可免去对该 sorted run 的访问。相比之下，range-lookup 旨在提取给定 key range 内的 entry。其过程与 point-lookup 类似，但有两点不同：(1) 不使用 Bloom filter；(2) 会访问每个 sorted run。

**读写权衡。** LSM-tree 中的“读写权衡”是 lookup 成本与 update 成本之间的平衡。通过改变结构，LSM-tree 可以针对 lookup 或 update 进行优化，但改善其中一方会以牺牲另一方为代价。不同增长方案会产生不同的权衡曲线，体现为了改善一种成本，必须牺牲另一种成本的程度。读写权衡更优的设计，在使其中一种成本达到某一性能水平时，只需对另一种成本作出更小让步。

sections.title: 3 Analysis of Growth Schemes
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Vertical Scheme.** In the vertical scheme, the capacity of each level is fixed, with a size ratio $T$ between two adjacent levels. A compaction happens when the data in the level reaches the predefined capacity, subsequently triggering a merge of the data in that level to the next level. A new level will be created to accommodate the compaction of the current largest level. As a result, the LSM-tree grows vertically (i.e., additional levels are gradually created) as the overall data volume expands. Given the buffer size $B$, the capacity of Level $i$ is set as $B · T^i$, a compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$ is triggered when the data volume at Level $i$ surpasses its capacity.

**Horizontal Scheme.** In the horizontal scheme, the number of levels is strictly fixed. Consequently, with the influx of more data, the LSM-tree broadens horizontally, meaning that each level’s capacity would increase over time. As shown in Algorithm 1, let $\ell$ be the maximum number of levels, and $C_i$ be the times of compactions conducted from Level $i - 1$ to Level $i$ since the previous compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$. Especially, for the first level, $C_1$ is the number of flushes from memory buffer to Level 1 since the previous compaction from Level 1 to Level 2. For the largest level, $C_\ell$ is the number of flushes from Level $\ell - 1$ to Level $\ell$. A full compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$ is triggered whenever $C_i > C_{i+1}$. Intuitively, the horizontal scheme dynamically controls the data volume ratio between two adjacent levels by their relative numbers of compactions.

*Fig. 2. Running examples of the vertical and horizontal growth schemes.*

**Running Example (Figure 2).** Let $n$ be the number of times a full memory buffer is flushed to disk. We set size ratio $T = 2$ for the vertical scheme, and let the maximum number of levels $\ell$ be 2 in the horizontal scheme. Initially, each level of the vertical scheme has a predetermined capacity, with the first level having a capacity that can hold 2 buffers, and the second level can hold 4 buffers. In contrast, the horizontal scheme’s levels do not have a predetermined capacity. Under the vertical scheme, when the number of buffer flushes $n = 1$, the LSM-tree consists solely of Level 1, accommodating data equivalent to the buffer size. When $n = 2$, the data volume of Level 1 reaches its capacity after two buffer flushes, and a compaction from Level 1 is triggered, with Level 2 created as the target level. The subsequent compaction from Level 1 to Level 2 occurs at $n = 4$, at which point Level 2 also reaches its capacity after two compactions from Level 1. Consequently, a new Level 3 is then established, enforcing a compaction from Level 2 to Level 3. In this way, LSM-tree under the vertical scheme continuously expands vertically. On the other hand, the horizontal scheme assigns each level a compaction counter, starting at 0. Each buffer flush increases the counter $C_1$ at Level 1 by one. After the first buffer flush ($n = 1$), we have $C_1 = 1$ and $C_2 = 0$. Given these values, the compaction condition for Level 1 under the horizontal scheme, $C_1 > C_2$, is met, thereby triggering a compaction from Level 1 to Level 2. After this compaction, $C_1$ is reset to 0, while $C_2$ increases by one. When $n = 3$, $C_1$ equals 2, which surpasses $C_2 = 1$, thus triggering another compaction from Level 1 to Level 2. Subsequently, $C_1$ is reset to 0 again, while $C_2$ rises to 2. A similar process recurs at $n = 6$.

**Algorithm 1: Horizontal Scheme**

*Input:* Maximum number of levels $\ell$

1. for $i = 1$ to $\ell$ do
2.     $C_i \leftarrow 0$
3. for each flush from buffer do
4.     $C_1 \leftarrow C_1 + 1$;
5.     for $i = 1$ to $\ell - 1$ do
6.         if $C_i > C_{i+1}$ then
7.             Trigger a full compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$;
8.             $C_{i+1} \leftarrow C_{i+1} + 1$, $C_i \leftarrow 0$;

*Fig. 3. Illustrating the cost of different schemes.*

**Horizontal Scheme Achieves a Better Read-Write trade-off (a.k.a., Bentley and Saxe’s theory [7]).** The compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$ involves merge-sorting all data from Level $i$ with existing data in Level $i + 1$, keeping each level as a single sorted run. This process becomes increasingly costly as the size of Level $i + 1$ grows, especially if equal amounts of data are merged each time, as is typical in the vertical scheme. For example, as shown in Figure 3 (a), the vertical scheme moves a total of 60MB data from Level 1 to Level 2 in three equal compactions of 20MB, which results in compaction costs of 20MB, 40MB, and 60MB, leading to a total of 120MB of writes. Intuitively, a more efficient approach is to perform compaction more frequently when Level 2 is smaller and less frequently as it grows. By merging at 10MB, 20MB, and then 30MB, the compaction costs become 10MB, 30MB, and 60MB, reducing the total write cost to 100MB, as illustrated in Figure 3 (b). This is precisely the strategy employed by the horizontal scheme.

In fact, Bentley and Saxe [7] (later refined by Mathieu et al. [45] for LSM-tree context) have demonstrated the optimality of the horizontal scheme, that the horizontal scheme incurs the minimal write cost for a given number of levels. Since the read cost of an LSM-tree under the leveling merge policy is proportional to the number of levels, the horizontal scheme consistently achieves the optimal trade-off between the read and write costs.

**Limitations of the Horizontal Scheme.** The previous analyses conclude that the traditional vertical scheme is suboptimal, while the horizontal scheme can provide better overall read-write trade-off. Nevertheless, as we mentioned, most recent key-value stores [12, 13, 20, 25, 33, 34, 53] tend to adopt the vertical scheme. This somewhat contradicts the theoretical results presented above. We conjecture that this discrepancy is probably due to two practical limitations of the horizontal scheme.

*Limited capability in handling update-heavy workloads.* Update-heavy workloads are common in practice. The vertical scheme effectively accommodates such workloads by switching to tiering merge policy. In contrast, the horizontal scheme has only been designed for the leveling merge policy. Moreover, as we will show shortly, directly applying the tiering merge policy may easily lead to sub-optimal performance. To address this limitation, we propose the horizontal-tiering scheme, which is the first horizontal scheme algorithm that can be effectively applied under the tiering merge policy. This approach will be explained in detail in Section 4.

*Gap between theory and practice.* Horizontal scheme is based on full compaction granularity, whereas file-level partial compaction granularity is the standard in most industrial systems, and has demonstrated practical advantages in certain aspects of empirical system performance, including significantly lower space amplification and improved worst-case throughput. This discrepancy arises because a single full compaction merges the entire contents of one level into the next level all at once. When the sizes of the participating levels are large, this process incurs substantial space amplification (since, for consistency, the data involved in compaction must be backed up until the compaction completes) and causes write stalls (as data in preceding levels cannot be transferred while compaction is underway). In contrast, each partial compaction merges only a single file from one level with a small set of overlapping files in the next level, thereby affecting only a fraction of the data and considerably mitigating these issues. This gap between theory and practice motivates us to propose a novel design that integrates both the horizontal and vertical scheme, as described in Section 5.

*Fig. 4. Different compactions lead to varying read costs.*
sections.translationMarkdown:
**垂直方案。** 在垂直方案中，每一层的容量固定，相邻两层之间的大小比为 $T$。当某层的数据达到预定义容量时，会发生 compaction，继而触发该层数据 merge 到下一层。当前最大层发生 compaction 时，会创建一个新层来容纳其输出。因此，随着总体数据量扩大，LSM-tree 会沿垂直方向增长（即逐步创建更多层）。给定 buffer 大小 $B$，Level $i$ 的容量设为 $B · T^i$；当 Level $i$ 的数据量超过其容量时，会触发从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的 compaction。

**水平方案。** 在水平方案中，层数被严格固定。因此，随着更多数据流入，LSM-tree 会沿水平方向扩展，也就是说每一层的容量会随时间增加。如算法 1 所示，令 $\ell$ 为最大层数，$C_i$ 表示自上一次从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的 compaction 以来，从 Level $i - 1$ 到 Level $i$ 已执行的 compaction 次数。特别地，对第一层而言，$C_1$ 是自上一次从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction 以来，从内存 buffer flush 到 Level 1 的次数。对最大层而言，$C_\ell$ 是从 Level $\ell - 1$ flush 到 Level $\ell$ 的次数。每当 $C_i > C_{i+1}$ 时，就会触发一次从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的 full compaction。直观而言，水平方案利用相邻两层的相对 compaction 次数，动态控制它们之间的数据量比。

*图 2. 垂直与水平增长方案的运行示例。*

**运行示例（图 2）。** 令 $n$ 表示一个填满的内存 buffer 被 flush 到磁盘的次数。对于垂直方案，我们将大小比设为 $T = 2$；对于水平方案，我们令最大层数 $\ell$ 为 2。初始时，垂直方案的每一层都有预定容量：第一层的容量可容纳 2 个 buffer，第二层可容纳 4 个 buffer。相比之下，水平方案中的层没有预定容量。在垂直方案下，当 buffer flush 次数 $n = 1$ 时，LSM-tree 仅由 Level 1 构成，其中容纳与 buffer 大小相等的数据。当 $n = 2$ 时，Level 1 的数据量在两次 buffer flush 后达到其容量，从而触发 Level 1 的 compaction，并创建 Level 2 作为目标层。下一次从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction 发生在 $n = 4$；此时，Level 2 在经历两次来自 Level 1 的 compaction 后也达到容量。因此，系统随即建立新的 Level 3，并强制执行一次从 Level 2 到 Level 3 的 compaction。通过这种方式，垂直方案下的 LSM-tree 不断沿垂直方向扩展。另一方面，水平方案为每一层分配一个初值为 0 的 compaction 计数器。每次 buffer flush 都会使 Level 1 的计数器 $C_1$ 加一。第一次 buffer flush（$n = 1$）后，有 $C_1 = 1$、$C_2 = 0$。根据这些值，水平方案中 Level 1 的 compaction 条件 $C_1 > C_2$ 得到满足，从而触发一次从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction。此次 compaction 后，$C_1$ 重置为 0，而 $C_2$ 加一。当 $n = 3$ 时，$C_1$ 等于 2，超过 $C_2 = 1$，因而再次触发从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction。随后，$C_1$ 再次重置为 0，而 $C_2$ 上升到 2。$n = 6$ 时会重复类似过程。

**算法 1：水平方案**

*输入：* 最大层数 $\ell$

1. for $i = 1$ to $\ell$ do
2.     $C_i \leftarrow 0$
3. 对每次来自 buffer 的 flush，执行
4.     $C_1 \leftarrow C_1 + 1$；
5.     for $i = 1$ to $\ell - 1$ do
6.         if $C_i > C_{i+1}$ then
7.             触发一次从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的 full compaction；
8.             $C_{i+1} \leftarrow C_{i+1} + 1$，$C_i \leftarrow 0$；

*图 3. 不同方案的成本示意。*

**水平方案实现了更优的读写权衡（亦即 Bentley 和 Saxe 理论 [7]）。** 从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的 compaction 会把 Level $i$ 中的全部数据与 Level $i + 1$ 的已有数据进行 merge-sort，使每一层保持为单个 sorted run。随着 Level $i + 1$ 的大小增长，这一过程会变得越来越昂贵；如果每次 merge 的数据量相等，情况尤其如此，而这正是垂直方案的典型做法。例如，如图 3(a) 所示，垂直方案通过三次大小均为 20MB 的 compaction，将总计 60MB 的数据从 Level 1 移至 Level 2；对应的 compaction 成本分别为 20MB、40MB 和 60MB，最终产生总计 120MB 的写入。直观而言，更高效的方法是在 Level 2 较小时更频繁地执行 compaction，并随着它增长而降低 compaction 频率。依次在数据量为 10MB、20MB、30MB 时进行 merge，compaction 成本将变为 10MB、30MB 和 60MB，从而把总写成本降至 100MB，如图 3(b) 所示。这恰好就是水平方案采用的策略。

事实上，Bentley 和 Saxe [7]（后来由 Mathieu 等人 [45] 针对 LSM-tree 场景加以完善）已经证明了水平方案的最优性：对于给定层数，水平方案产生的写成本最小。由于采用 leveling merge policy 的 LSM-tree 的读成本与层数成正比，因此水平方案始终能够实现读成本和写成本之间的最优权衡。

**水平方案的局限。** 前述分析得出结论：传统垂直方案是次优的，而水平方案可以提供更优的整体读写权衡。尽管如此，如前所述，近期的大多数 key-value store [12, 13, 20, 25, 33, 34, 53] 都倾向于采用垂直方案。这在某种程度上与上述理论结果相矛盾。我们推测，这种差异可能源于水平方案在实践中的两项局限。

*处理 update-heavy workload 的能力有限。* Update-heavy workload 在实践中很常见。垂直方案可以通过切换到 tiering merge policy 有效适应这类 workload。相比之下，水平方案只针对 leveling merge policy 设计。此外，正如我们即将展示的，直接应用 tiering merge policy 很容易导致次优性能。为解决这一局限，我们提出 horizontal-tiering 方案，它是第一个能够在 tiering merge policy 下有效应用的水平方案算法。第 4 节将详细说明该方法。

*理论与实践之间的差距。* 水平方案基于 full compaction 粒度，而文件级 partial compaction 粒度是多数工业系统中的标准，并且已在实际系统性能的某些方面展现出实践优势，包括显著降低空间放大和改善最坏情况下的吞吐量。产生这种差异的原因是，单次 full compaction 会一次性把某一层的全部内容 merge 到下一层。当参与 compaction 的层很大时，这一过程会造成显著的空间放大（因为为保证一致性，参与 compaction 的数据必须备份到 compaction 完成）和写停顿（因为 compaction 进行期间，前面各层中的数据无法继续向下转移）。相比之下，每次 partial compaction 只会将某一层的一个文件与下一层中一小组范围重叠的文件进行 merge，因此只影响一部分数据，并能大幅缓解这些问题。理论与实践之间的这一差距，促使我们提出一种同时融合水平与垂直方案的新设计，如第 5 节所述。

*图 4. 不同 compaction 会导致不同的读成本。*

sections.title: 4 The Horizontal-Tiering Scheme
sections.originalMarkdown: In this section, we introduce the horizontal-tiering scheme, which serves as the backbone of our proposed method, Vertiorizon. The horizontal-tiering scheme redefines the horizontal algorithm by integrating the tiering merge policy, which governs when the data in a level is compacted into the subsequent level as a new isolated sorted run. While there are numerous compaction-timing choices in an LSM-tree, our proposed horizontal-tiering scheme employs a compaction sequence that achieves the optimal read-write performance trade-off, or precisely, achieves the minimum write cost when fixing the number of levels. This is the first significant extension of the horizontal growth scheme to the tiering merge policy, resulting in a much broader range of optimal read-write performance trade-offs for horizontal schemes. Although the proof of these optimality properties is quite intricate, the algorithm of the final scheme is easy to implement, making it appealing from an engineering standpoint as well.
sections.translationMarkdown: 本节介绍 horizontal-tiering 方案，它是我们所提出方法 Vertiorizon 的基础。Horizontal-tiering 方案通过集成 tiering merge policy 重新定义了水平算法；该策略决定何时将某一层中的数据 compact 到下一层，并形成一个新的、独立的 sorted run。尽管 LSM-tree 中存在众多 compaction 时机选择，但我们提出的 horizontal-tiering 方案采用了一种能够实现最优读写性能权衡的 compaction 序列；更准确地说，在层数固定时，它实现了最低写成本。这是首次将水平增长方案显著扩展到 tiering merge policy，使水平方案能够覆盖广泛得多的最优读写性能权衡范围。虽然这些最优性性质的证明相当复杂，但最终方案的算法易于实现，因此从工程角度看也很有吸引力。

sections.title: 4.1 High-Level Idea
sections.originalMarkdown:
Under the tiering merge policy, we first claim that the write amplification, in the long run, is the same regardless of when we compact data from a level to its subsequent level. This is because, with the tiering policy, each entry incurs only a single write amplification (i.e., rewritten onto the disk once) when passing through each level, exactly at the time when it is moved onto the level. As a result, the key to optimizing overall performance lies in selecting the optimal timing for compacting data from Level $i$ into a new run at Level $i + 1$, in order to minimize read amplification.

A simple approach is to follow the vertical scheme by using the original Algorithm 1, but instead of merge-sorting data with the existing content in Level $i + 1$, the compaction would simply move data from Level $i$ into a new run in Level $i + 1$. However, this naive strategy would result in significantly higher read amplification, because it would quickly create many runs in a level, even when the total data size in that level is still relatively small. Since the read cost is roughly proportional to the number of runs, this approach would lead to suboptimal read performance. This underscores the critical role of compaction timing in optimizing read efficiency.

To further explore the complexity of this challenge, we consider three examples in Figure 4, where a total of 60MB of data is moved from Level 1 to Level 2 across 3 compactions as data is written into the workload. We further assume there are $x$ lookups when 1MB of data is ingested, and each lookup incurs an I/O for every run present at that time.[^1] Figure 4 (a) describes an equal-frequency compaction, where a compaction from Level 1 to Level 2 happens every insertion of 20MB data. Figure 4 (b) illustrates an increasing-frequency compaction strategy, where compactions occur after ingesting 30MB, 20MB, and 10MB of data, respectively. In contrast, Figure 4 (c) presents an extreme case where three compactions happen consecutively after inserting 60MB of data. Now, consider the total read cost during the insertion of 60MB. We will analyze the costs at Level 1 and Level 2 separately.

At Level 1, each run is 10MB in size, and if compactions are less frequent, earlier runs remain in the level for longer, increasing the read cost. Thus, Case (c) results in significantly more I/Os at Level 1 (150 times of I/O operations), compared to Case (a) and Case (b), which incur 30 and 40 times of I/O operations, respectively. However, compacting more frequently increases the cost at Level 2, as runs are generated earlier and serve more lookups. For example, Case (a) has a higher cost at Level 2 because runs stay longer in this level compared to the other two cases. Among the three, Case (b) achieves the lowest overall I/O cost. Its first compaction delayed until 30MB of data has been written, and subsequent compactions occur more frequently, triggered after 20MB and 10MB of written data, respectively. In this manner, by the third compaction, the two earlier sorted runs in Level 2 have existed for shorter duration compared to the case in Figure 4 (a). The first run has only undergone 30 times of lookups, while the second run has experienced just 10 times of lookups, as compared to 40 and 20 times of lookups in Case (a). This suggests that a decreasing-frequency compaction strategy may help reduce read amplification.

Building on this insight, an intuitive approach is to perform compactions to Level $i$ less frequently when it is relatively empty, because the sorted runs written at this stage will exist for a longer period and thus contribute more to read amplification. In contrast, when Level $i$ is close to full, we can add new sorted runs at shorter intervals, since they will not persist long and will therefore have a smaller impact on read amplification. We formalize the insight into developing the horizontal-tiering scheme below.

[^1]: For simplicity, this assumption excludes common memory optimizations typically employed in LSM-trees, such as Bloom filters and block caches. However, our conclusions remain valid even when considering these optimizations. For example, in an LSM-tree utilizing Bloom filters and block caches, if the workload is uniform, there is still a consistent probability (approximately equal to the Bloom filter’s false positive rate) that a data block retrieval will occur on each run during every lookup. In this context, the insight presented in this example continues to hold.
sections.translationMarkdown:
在 tiering merge policy 下，我们首先提出：从长期来看，无论何时将某一层的数据 compact 到下一层，写放大都是相同的。这是因为，在 tiering 策略下，每个 entry 在通过每一层时只会产生一次写放大（即在磁盘上重写一次），并且恰好发生在它被移动到该层时。因此，为了最小化读放大，优化整体性能的关键在于：选择将数据从 Level $i$ compact 到 Level $i + 1$ 中一个新 run 的最佳时机。

一种简单方法是沿用垂直方案，使用原始 Algorithm 1；但 compaction 不再将数据与 Level $i + 1$ 中的现有内容进行归并排序，而只是将数据从 Level $i$ 移入 Level $i + 1$ 中的一个新 run。然而，这种朴素策略会导致显著更高的读放大，因为即使某一层中的数据总量仍然相对较小，它也会迅速在该层创建许多 run。由于读成本大致与 run 的数量成正比，这种方法会造成次优的读性能。这凸显了 compaction 时机对于优化读取效率的关键作用。

为了进一步探究这一挑战的复杂性，我们考虑 Figure 4 中的三个例子：随着数据写入 workload，共有 60MB 数据通过 3 次 compaction 从 Level 1 移动到 Level 2。我们进一步假设，每摄取 1MB 数据就会发生 $x$ 次 lookup，并且每次 lookup 都会针对当时存在的每个 run 产生一次 I/O。[^1] Figure 4 (a) 描述了等频率 compaction，其中每插入 20MB 数据，就会发生一次从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction。Figure 4 (b) 展示了一种频率逐渐增加的 compaction 策略，其中三次 compaction 分别在摄取 30MB、20MB 和 10MB 数据后发生。相比之下，Figure 4 (c) 给出了一个极端情形：插入 60MB 数据后，连续发生三次 compaction。现在考虑插入这 60MB 数据期间的总读成本。我们将分别分析 Level 1 和 Level 2 的成本。

在 Level 1 中，每个 run 的大小为 10MB；如果 compaction 频率较低，较早产生的 run 会在该层停留更长时间，从而增加读成本。因此，与 Case (a) 和 Case (b) 分别产生 30 次和 40 次 I/O 操作相比，Case (c) 在 Level 1 产生的 I/O 要多得多（150 次 I/O 操作）。然而，更频繁地执行 compaction 会增加 Level 2 的成本，因为 run 会更早生成并服务更多次 lookup。例如，与另外两种情况相比，Case (a) 中的 run 在 Level 2 停留得更久，因此该层成本更高。在三种情况中，Case (b) 实现了最低的总体 I/O 成本。它的第一次 compaction 延迟到写入 30MB 数据之后，随后 compaction 发生得更加频繁，分别在再写入 20MB 和 10MB 数据后触发。通过这种方式，到第三次 compaction 时，Level 2 中较早生成的两个 sorted run 的存在时间比 Figure 4 (a) 中更短。第一个 run 只经历了 30 次 lookup，第二个 run 仅经历了 10 次 lookup，而 Case (a) 中对应的次数分别为 40 次和 20 次。这表明，频率递减的 compaction 策略可能有助于降低读放大。

基于这一观察，一种直观方法是：当 Level $i$ 相对较空时，以较低频率向其中执行 compaction，因为在这一阶段写入的 sorted run 会存在更长时间，从而对读放大贡献更大。相反，当 Level $i$ 接近填满时，我们可以用更短的间隔添加新的 sorted run，因为它们不会长期存在，对读放大的影响也因此较小。下面，我们将这一观察形式化，并据此构建 horizontal-tiering 方案。

[^1]: 为了简化分析，这一假设排除了 LSM-tree 中通常采用的常见内存优化，例如 Bloom filter 和 block cache。然而，即使考虑这些优化，我们的结论依然成立。例如，在使用 Bloom filter 和 block cache 的 LSM-tree 中，如果 workload 是均匀的，那么每次 lookup 时，仍然存在一个固定概率（近似等于 Bloom filter 的 false positive rate）会在每个 run 上发生一次数据块读取。在这种情况下，该例子所表达的观察仍然成立。

sections.title: 4.2 Algorithms
sections.originalMarkdown:
Building upon our previous analysis, we introduce a new horizontal-tiering scheme that extends the original horizontal scheme to better accommodate update-heavy workloads, providing a superior read-write trade-off compared to vertical-tiering. As shown in Algorithm 3, the overall algorithm is similar to the default horizontal scheme under leveling, with two key differences. First, the compaction counters of all levels are initialized to an integer $k$ instead of zero (Lines 4–5). Second, when a buffer flush or compaction occurs, the compaction counter for the target level decrements instead of incrementing. Furthermore, a level’s compaction is triggered when its counter reaches zero, and after compaction, its counter is reset to the current counter value of its subsequent level (Lines 6–12).

These two differences cause compactions to become increasingly frequent as the counters decrease. To illustrate this point, we present a simple example in Figure 5, where $\ell = 2$ and $k = 3$, meaning that all levels’ compaction counters are initialized to 3. With each buffer flush, the compaction counter $C_1$ at Level 1 decreases by 1. When $n = 3$ (after three buffer flushes), $C_1$ is reduced to zero and meets the compaction condition $C_i = 0$, which triggers a compaction from Level 1 to Level 2. Following this compaction, $C_2$ decreases by 1, and $C_1$ is reset to the current value of $C_2$, now 2. After two additional buffer flushes, $C_1$ decreases to 0 once again, triggering another compaction. Under the tiering merge policy, the data from Level 1 is merged into a new sorted run in Level 2. Subsequently, $C_2$ decreases from 2 to 1, and $C_1$ is reset to 1. The next compaction then occurs after only one buffer flush. As $C_2$ decreases due to compactions, the compactions from Level 1 to Level 2 become increasingly frequent. This behavior aligns with the best-performing increasing-frequency compaction strategy depicted in Figure 4(b).

*Fig. 5. Running example of the horizontal-tiering scheme.*

**Algorithm 2: Horizontal-tiering Scheme**

**Input:** Maximum number of levels $\ell$, data size $N$

1. $k \leftarrow 0$;
2. **while** $\binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell} < \frac{N}{B}$ **do**
3.     $k \leftarrow k + 1$
4. **for** $i = 1$ **to** $\ell$ **do**
5.     $C_i \leftarrow k$
6. **for each** flush from buffer **do**
7.     $C_1 \leftarrow C_1 - 1$;
8.     **for** $i = 1$ **to** $\ell - 1$ **do**
9.         **if** $C_i = 0$ **then**
10.             Trigger a full compaction from Level $i$ to Level $i + 1$;
11.             $C_{i+1} \leftarrow C_{i+1} - 1$;
12.             **for** $j = 1$ **to** $i$ **do**
13.                 $C_j \leftarrow C_{i+1}$;

The algorithm initializes the compaction counters for all levels to $k$ (Lines 1–5), where $k$ is the smallest integer satisfying the inequality $\frac{N}{B} \leq \binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell}$. The reason for such setting is as follows. In running the algorithm, the compaction counters for each level will gradually decrease, until the compaction counters of all levels reach zero. Therefore, the initial value of the compaction counter must be set based on the total data size $N$[^2], where a larger data size corresponds to a larger initial compaction counter value $k$. We present a novel lemma to formalize the relationship between $k$ and the data size, which explains the initial setting of $k$.

**Lemma 4.1.** *Under the horizontal-tiering scheme, if we initially set the compaction counters of all levels to $k$, then after $\binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell}$ buffer flushes, the compaction counters of all levels will decrease to zero.*

[^2]: Specifically, $N$ represents the data size that the LSM-tree will have written by the end of Algorithm 2. In practical applications, $N$ may be set according to the capacity of the storage device or a more precise value based on user estimates. In general, as long as $N$ falls within a reasonable range, deviations in its value do not significantly affect the practical performance of Algorithm 2.
sections.translationMarkdown:
基于前面的分析，我们提出一种新的 horizontal-tiering 方案。它扩展了原始水平方案，以更好地适应 update-heavy workload；与 vertical-tiering 相比，它能提供更优的读写权衡。如 Algorithm 3 所示，整体算法与 leveling 下的默认水平方案类似，但存在两个关键差异。第一，所有层的 compaction 计数器都被初始化为整数 $k$，而不是零（Lines 4–5）。第二，当发生 buffer flush 或 compaction 时，目标层的 compaction 计数器会递减而非递增。此外，当某一层的计数器降到零时，就会触发该层的 compaction；compaction 完成后，该计数器被重置为其下一层计数器的当前值（Lines 6–12）。

随着计数器递减，这两个差异会使 compaction 变得越来越频繁。为了说明这一点，我们在 Figure 5 中给出了一个简单例子，其中 $\ell = 2$、$k = 3$，这意味着所有层的 compaction 计数器都初始化为 3。每发生一次 buffer flush，Level 1 的 compaction 计数器$C_1$ 就减 1。当 $n = 3$ 时（即三次 buffer flush 之后），$C_1$ 降为零，满足 compaction 条件 $C_i = 0$，从而触发一次从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction。该次 compaction 之后，$C_2$ 减 1，而 $C_1$ 被重置为 $C_2$ 的当前值，即 2。再发生两次 buffer flush 后，$C_1$ 再次降为 0，从而触发另一次 compaction。在 tiering merge policy 下，Level 1 中的数据被合并为 Level 2 中一个新的 sorted run。随后，$C_2$ 从 2 降为 1，而 $C_1$ 被重置为 1。下一次 compaction 只需再发生一次 buffer flush 就会触发。随着 compaction 使 $C_2$ 不断减小，从 Level 1 到 Level 2 的 compaction 会变得越来越频繁。这一行为与 Figure 4(b) 所示、表现最佳的频率递增 compaction 策略一致。

*图 5. Horizontal-tiering 方案的运行示例。*

**Algorithm 2：Horizontal-tiering 方案**

**输入：**最大层数 $\ell$，数据大小 $N$

1. $k \leftarrow 0$；
2. **当** $\binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell} < \frac{N}{B}$ **时循环**
3.     $k \leftarrow k + 1$
4. **对** $i = 1$ **到** $\ell$ **执行**
5.     $C_i \leftarrow k$
6. **对 buffer 的每次 flush 执行**
7.     $C_1 \leftarrow C_1 - 1$；
8.     **对** $i = 1$ **到** $\ell - 1$ **执行**
9.         **如果** $C_i = 0$，**则**
10.             触发一次从 Level $i$ 到 Level $i + 1$ 的 full compaction；
11.             $C_{i+1} \leftarrow C_{i+1} - 1$；
12.             **对** $j = 1$ **到** $i$ **执行**
13.                 $C_j \leftarrow C_{i+1}$；

该算法将所有层的 compaction 计数器初始化为 $k$（Lines 1–5），其中 $k$ 是满足不等式 $\frac{N}{B} \leq \binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell}$ 的最小整数。这样设置的原因如下。在算法运行过程中，每一层的 compaction 计数器都会逐渐减小，直到所有层的 compaction 计数器都降为零。因此，compaction 计数器的初始值必须根据总数据大小 $N$[^2] 设置；数据越大，初始 compaction 计数器值 $k$ 就越大。我们提出一个新的引理来形式化 $k$ 与数据大小之间的关系，并解释 $k$ 的初始设置。

**Lemma 4.1.** *在 horizontal-tiering 方案下，如果我们最初将所有层的 compaction 计数器都设置为 $k$，那么经过 $\binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell}$ 次 buffer flush 后，所有层的 compaction 计数器都会降为零。*

[^2]: 具体而言，$N$ 表示到 Algorithm 2 结束时，LSM-tree 已经写入的数据大小。在实际应用中，可以根据存储设备容量设置 $N$，也可以根据用户估计设置一个更精确的值。一般而言，只要 $N$ 处于合理范围内，其取值偏差就不会显著影响 Algorithm 2 的实际性能。

sections.title: 4.3 Optimality Analysis
sections.originalMarkdown:
The traditional horizontal scheme is known to achieve the optimal read-write trade-off under the leveling merge policy. Our proposed horizontal-tiering algorithm generalizes this optimality to the tiering merge policy. As we mentioned, the optimality analysis can be reduced to analyzing the total read cost, when the number of levels is fixed. Next, we show that the compaction sequence described in Algorithm 2 achieves the optimal read cost.

We first state the problem setting as follows. Between consecutive buffer flushes, compactions can be performed between adjacent levels to control the number of runs of Level 1 and other levels, reducing future write costs. A compaction that starts from Level $l_1$ and ends at Level $l_2$ would merge all runs from Levels $l_1$, $l_1 + 1$, ..., $l_2 - 1$ into Level $l_2$. Different growth schemes result in varying compaction timings, which ultimately lead to different read overheads. For example, if compactions are too infrequent or not performed at all, sorted runs accumulate in Level 1, making lookups increasingly costly. The timing of each compaction can be represented by a triple $p = (I, l_1, l_2)$, where $I$ denotes that the compaction occurs after the $I$-th buffer flush, and $l_1$ and $l_2$ indicate the starting and ending levels, respectively. This scenario can then be formulated into the following problem.

**Problem 1.** *Consider a workload that would incur a total of $n$ sequential buffer flushes in the LSM-tree, which has a maximum of $\ell$ levels. Our objective is to find the optimal compaction sequence $S^* = \{p_1^*, p_2^*, ...\}$ to minimize the total read cost of processing this workload. We define this problem as $\psi(n, \ell)$. *

In a tiered LSM-tree, different growth schemes correspond to specific compaction sequences in Problem 1, resulting in varying costs. We give the following theorem stating that the compaction sequence employed by our horizontal-tiering algorithm incurs the minimal cost. Here we provide only a proof sketch, and the detailed proof is available in our technical report [1].

**Theorem 4.2.** *When there are total data of size $N = \binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell} \cdot B$ written into the LSM-tree, the compaction operations in the horizontal-tiering scheme described in Algorithm 2 is optimal for Problem 1.*

**Proof Sketch.** We can adopt a dynamic programming approach to solve Problem 1 by decomposing it into smaller subproblems. Consider the problem $\psi(n, \ell)$, where $n$ is the number of buffer flushes and $\ell$ is the number of levels. Suppose that in the optimal compaction sequence $S^* = \{p_1^*, p_2^*, ...\}$, the first compaction to the largest level $p_f^*$ occurs after the $i$-th buffer flush. We can then partition the original problem into two subproblems – Subproblem $\psi(i, \ell - 1)$ that addresses the first $i$ buffer flushes with the first $\ell - 1$ levels, and subproblem $\psi(n - i, \ell)$ that involves the remaining $n - i$ buffer flushes and all $\ell$ levels. If the optimal compaction sequences for these subproblems are $S_1$ and $S_2$ respectively, then the optimal solution to the original problem satisfies $S^* = \{S_1, p_f^*, S_2\}$.

We first explain why the portion of $S^*$ preceding $p_l^*$ must be $S_1$. Before the first compaction to the largest level in $S^*$, the data entering the LSM-tree with the first $i$ buffer flushes remains within the first $\ell - 1$ levels since no compaction to Level $\ell$ occurs before $p_f^*$. This scenario exactly matches problem $\psi(n - i, \ell)$ because $S^*$ is the optimal compaction sequence that minimizes the total read cost, and the portion of $S^*$ preceding $p_f^*$ must correspond to the optimal solution of $\psi(i, \ell - 1)$. Otherwise, we could replace that portion with the optimal sequence $S_1$, resulting in a lower total read cost, contradiction ensues. Similarly, the portion of $S^*$ following $p_f^*$ must correspond to the optimal solution of $\psi(n - i, \ell)$, since the portion of data already moved into the largest level would not be merged with and affect any further data entering the LSM-tree. If it were not optimal, substituting it with $S_2$ would yield a compaction sequence with a lower read cost. By recursively applying this decomposition, we can solve Problem 1 by breaking it down into subproblems until they become trivial (e.g., when $n = 1$ or $\ell = 1$). This dynamic programming approach constructs the optimal compaction sequence by combining the solutions of all these subproblems.

Furthermore, we can prove the following claim by mathematical induction. For any $n$ and $\ell$, if in the optimal compaction sequence corresponding to $\psi(n, \ell)$, the last compaction to the largest level occurs after the $i$-th buffer flush, then $i$ must satisfy

$$
\binom{m-1}{\ell} \leq n-i \leq \binom{m}{\ell}, \quad \text{and} \quad \binom{m-1}{\ell-1} \leq i \leq \binom{m}{\ell-1} \tag{1}
$$

where $m$ is the integer that satisfies $\binom{m}{\ell} \leq n \leq \binom{m+1}{\ell}$. Having identified the corresponding timing of compaction $p_f^*$, we can divide any problem into two subproblems. For each of these subproblems, we apply this equation to determine their first compaction to the largest level $p_f^{\prime *}$. This recursive process continues until all resulting subproblems can no longer be subdivided, that is, when either $n = 1$ or $\ell = 1$. The collection of all compactions determined through this process constitutes an optimal compaction sequence, which matches the compaction operations in Algorithm 2. $\square$
sections.translationMarkdown:
众所周知，在 leveling merge policy 下，传统水平方案能够实现最优的读写权衡。我们提出的 horizontal-tiering 算法将这一最优性推广到了 tiering merge policy。如前所述，当层数固定时，最优性分析可以归约为对总读成本的分析。接下来，我们将证明 Algorithm 2 所描述的 compaction 序列能够实现最优读成本。

首先给出问题设定。在连续两次 buffer flush 之间，可以在相邻层之间执行 compaction，以控制 Level 1 和其他层中的 run 数量，从而降低未来的写成本。一次从 Level $l_1$ 开始、在 Level $l_2$ 结束的 compaction，会将 Levels $l_1$, $l_1 + 1$, ..., $l_2 - 1$ 中的所有 run 合并到 Level $l_2$。不同增长方案会产生不同的 compaction 时机，并最终导致不同的读开销。例如，如果 compaction 过于稀疏，或者完全不执行 compaction，sorted run 就会在 Level 1 中累积，使 lookup 的成本越来越高。每次 compaction 的时机可以用三元组 $p = (I, l_1, l_2)$ 表示，其中 $I$ 表示 compaction 在第 $I$ 次 buffer flush 之后发生，$l_1$ 和 $l_2$ 分别表示起始层和终止层。于是，可以将这一情形形式化为以下问题。

**Problem 1.** *考虑一个 workload，它会在最大层数为 $\ell$ 的 LSM-tree 中导致总计 $n$ 次连续的 buffer flush。我们的目标是找到最优 compaction 序列 $S^* = \{p_1^*, p_2^*, ...\}$，以最小化处理该 workload 的总读成本。我们将这一问题定义为 $\psi(n, \ell)$。*

在 tiered LSM-tree 中，不同增长方案对应于 Problem 1 中特定的 compaction 序列，从而产生不同的成本。下面给出的定理表明，我们的 horizontal-tiering 算法所采用的 compaction 序列具有最小成本。这里仅给出证明概要，详细证明可见我们的技术报告 [1]。

**Theorem 4.2.** *当写入 LSM-tree 的数据总大小为 $N = \binom{k+\ell-1}{\ell} \cdot B$ 时，Algorithm 2 所描述的 horizontal-tiering 方案中的 compaction 操作对于 Problem 1 是最优的。*

**Proof Sketch.** 我们可以采用动态规划方法，将 Problem 1 分解为更小的子问题来求解。考虑问题 $\psi(n, \ell)$，其中 $n$ 是 buffer flush 的次数，$\ell$ 是层数。假设在最优 compaction 序列 $S^* = \{p_1^*, p_2^*, ...\}$ 中，第一次对最大层执行的 compaction $p_f^*$ 发生在第 $i$ 次 buffer flush 之后。于是，可以将原问题划分为两个子问题：Subproblem $\psi(i, \ell - 1)$ 使用前 $\ell - 1$ 层处理前 $i$ 次 buffer flush；subproblem $\psi(n - i, \ell)$ 使用全部 $\ell$ 层处理剩余的 $n - i$ 次 buffer flush。若这两个子问题的最优 compaction 序列分别为 $S_1$ 和 $S_2$，则原问题的最优解满足 $S^* = \{S_1, p_f^*, S_2\}$。

我们首先解释为什么 $S^*$ 中位于 $p_l^*$ 之前的部分必须是 $S_1$。在 $S^*$ 中第一次对最大层执行 compaction 之前，由前 $i$ 次 buffer flush 进入 LSM-tree 的数据仍然位于前 $\ell - 1$ 层内，因为在 $p_f^*$ 之前没有对 Level $\ell$ 执行 compaction。这一情形与问题 $\psi(n - i, \ell)$ 完全一致，因为 $S^*$ 是使总读成本最小的最优 compaction 序列，而 $S^*$ 中位于 $p_f^*$ 之前的部分必须对应于 $\psi(i, \ell - 1)$ 的最优解。否则，我们可以用最优序列 $S_1$ 替换该部分，从而得到更低的总读成本，这就产生了矛盾。类似地，$S^*$ 中位于 $p_f^*$ 之后的部分必须对应于 $\psi(n - i, \ell)$ 的最优解，因为已经移入最大层的那部分数据不会与之后进入 LSM-tree 的任何数据合并，也不会影响它们。如果该部分不是最优的，用 $S_2$ 替换它就会得到读成本更低的 compaction 序列。通过递归应用这一分解过程，可以不断将 Problem 1 分解为子问题，直到子问题变得平凡（例如 $n = 1$ 或 $\ell = 1$）。这种动态规划方法通过组合所有这些子问题的解来构造最优 compaction 序列。

此外，我们可以通过数学归纳法证明以下命题。对于任意 $n$ 和 $\ell$，如果在与 $\psi(n, \ell)$ 对应的最优 compaction 序列中，最后一次对最大层执行的 compaction 发生在第 $i$ 次 buffer flush 之后，那么 $i$ 必须满足

$$
\binom{m-1}{\ell} \leq n-i \leq \binom{m}{\ell}, \quad \text{且} \quad \binom{m-1}{\ell-1} \leq i \leq \binom{m}{\ell-1} \tag{1}
$$

其中，$m$ 是满足 $\binom{m}{\ell} \leq n \leq \binom{m+1}{\ell}$ 的整数。确定对应的 compaction 时机 $p_f^*$ 后，我们可以将任意问题划分为两个子问题。对于每个子问题，我们都应用该方程来确定其中第一次对最大层执行的 compaction $p_f^{\prime *}$。这一递归过程会持续进行，直到所有得到的子问题都无法再被划分，也就是 $n = 1$ 或 $\ell = 1$ 时为止。通过这一过程确定的所有 compaction 构成一个最优 compaction 序列，该序列与 Algorithm 2 中的 compaction 操作相匹配。$\square$

sections.title: 5 The Vertiorizon Growth Scheme
sections.originalMarkdown: In the previous section, we introduced the enhanced horizontal growing scheme, which maintains high efficiency under update-heavy workloads. However, it still suffers from high space amplification and write stalls due to its reliance on full compaction. This limitation prompts us to propose Vertiorizon, a novel LSM growing scheme that combines both horizontal and vertical approaches to provide robust and comprehensive performance.
sections.translationMarkdown: 在上一节中，我们介绍了增强的水平增长方案，它在更新密集型 workload 下仍能保持较高效率。然而，由于依赖 full compaction，该方案仍会产生较高的空间放大和写停顿。这一局限促使我们提出 Vertiorizon：一种新颖的 LSM 增长方案，它结合水平与垂直两种方法，以提供稳健而全面的性能。

sections.title: 5.1 Overall Layout
sections.originalMarkdown:
Vertiorizon divides the LSM-tree into two sections from top to bottom – horizontal part and vertical part, such that the upper horizontal part utilizes the horizontal scheme for improving the overall performance, while the lower vertical part manages the largest two levels, aiming to mitigate space amplification and alleviate severe write stalls through partial compaction.

**Horizontal Part.** This part comprises the upper levels of the LSM-tree, where the vast majority of compactions occur. These levels follow the horizontal growth scheme to achieve better efficiency. The horizontal part has a predetermined capacity, which is $n$ times the buffer size (we will discuss how to set $n$ shortly). When the data size reaches this capacity, a full compaction is performed to merge all data into the vertical part. The structure of the horizontal part is flexible such that it can be adjusted according to the characteristics of the workload. Specifically, it has two configurable knobs. (1) Merge Policy: this allows switching between a leveling merge policy and a tiering merge policy. (2) Number of Levels $\ell$: under the leveling policy, a smaller number of levels leads to better read performance; under the tiering policy, fewer levels result in better write performance. Section 5.2 describes how Vertiorizon self-tunes these parameters.

**Vertical Part.** The vertical part comprises the two largest levels of the LSM-tree, each possessing fixed capacities similar to those in the vertical scheme. When the horizontal part reaches its total capacity, all data is merged into the first level of the vertical part through full compaction. Once the first level becomes full, data is moved to the second level—the largest level—via partial compaction. Like the horizontal part, the vertical part can switch between leveling and tiering merge policies and includes an adjustable parameter, i.e., the size ratio $T$ between adjacent levels in the vertical scheme. Specifically, by default, the capacity of the first level in the vertical part is $T$ times the total size of the horizontal part, which is $n \cdot T \cdot B$. The second level’s capacity is $T$ times that of the first level, resulting in $n \cdot T^2 \cdot B$. Because partial compaction between the two levels in the vertical part causes only minimal space amplification and write stalls, Vertiorizon can roughly reduce space amplification and write stalls by a factor of $T$ compared to an LSM-tree that employs full compaction throughout.

One may wonder why we empirically fix two levels in the vertical part. The rationale behind this is that the primary purpose of the vertical part is to avoid excessive space amplification and write stalls by enabling partial compaction between the largest levels By configuring the largest two levels to use the vertical scheme and applying partial compaction between them, Vertiorizon can significantly reduce space amplification and write stalls by roughly a factor of $T$ compared to an LSM-tree that employs full compaction throughout. We found that practically such setting is quite sufficient and effective. Further increasing the number of levels in the vertical part would require proportionally reducing the scale of the horizontal part, thereby diminishing its benefits. Note that, even though the number of levels in the vertical part is fixed, we can still trade between write amplification and space amplification by tuning the parameter $T$. Increasing $T$ can further decrease overall space amplification and write stalls but at the cost of increased write amplification in the vertical part.

**Running Example.** Figure 6 displays a typical process of Vertiorizon when ingesting new data. Initially, the horizontal part is ready to grow, while the first level of the vertical part holds data volume near its full capacity. Once the horizontal part is full, it starts a compaction to the first vertical level. This process empties all the horizontal levels. Subsequently, as the data volume in the first vertical level exceeds its capacity, data begins to gradually move into the largest level via partial compactions, after which the horizontal part begins a new phase of growth.

*Fig. 6. General process in Vertiorizon.*

**Dynamic Resizing of Horizontal Part.** To maintain the structure of the LSM-tree as the data volume scales up, Vertiorizon dynamically increases the parameter $n$, which controls the capacity of the horizontal part. Specifically, $n$ is initiated with a moderate value according to the data scale. When the largest level of the vertical part reaches its capacity, we increment $n$ by a factor of $1/T$ after the next full compaction clears the horizontal part, and adjust the capacity of the vertical part accordingly. This adjustment incurs no additional overhead because it is after a full clear of the horizontal part. In this way, the overall capacity of the LSM-tree under Vertiorizon can gradually expand.

**Optimize Write Amplification by Adjusting Size Ratio.** We can further optimize Vertiorizon’s write amplification by adjusting the size ratio of the two largest levels in the vertical part. These two levels are special because they transition from a level employing full compaction to that with partial compaction, opening up an opportunity to optimize the overall write amplification.

Since the vertical part employs partial compaction, the data volume in its first level consistently remains close to its capacity. Consequently, the compaction from the horizontal part to the first level of the vertical part will always incur a write amplification factor of $T$, because the data volume in this level is maintained at $T$ times the capacity of the horizontal part. In contrast, the average write amplification incurred when compacting from the first level to the second level of the vertical part is only $(T+1)/2$. This reduction occurs because partial compaction leads to uneven data density within the level, as established in previous studies [35]. Due to these differences, setting equal size ratios of $T$ results in different write amplifications for compactions at the two levels of the vertical part, leading to suboptimal overall write amplification. Specifically, we set the size ratio between the horizontal part and the first level of the vertical part to $T'$, and the size ratio between the first and second levels of the vertical part to $T^2/T'$. This setting ensures that the capacity of the largest level in Vertiorizon remains unchanged, thereby maintaining the overall scale of the LSM-tree. Under this setup, the write amplification at the first level of the vertical part becomes $T'$, while the write amplification at the second level is adjusted to $(T^2/T' + 1)/2$. Note that

$$
T' + \frac{T^2/T' + 1}{2} \geq 2\sqrt{T' \cdot \frac{T^2}{2T'}} + \frac{1}{2} = \sqrt{2}T + \frac{1}{2}. \tag{2}
$$

The above inequality is due to AM-GM Inequality, and the equality holds when $T' = T/\sqrt{2}$.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Vertiorizon 将 LSM-tree 自上而下划分为两个部分——水平部分和垂直部分。上方的水平部分采用水平方案来提升整体性能；下方的垂直部分管理最大的两个 level，旨在通过 partial compaction 缓解空间放大与严重的写停顿。

**水平部分。** 该部分由 LSM-tree 上方的若干 level 构成，绝大多数 compaction 都发生在这里。这些 level 遵循水平增长方案，以获得更高的效率。水平部分具有预先确定的容量，为 buffer 大小的 $n$ 倍（稍后将讨论如何设置 $n$）。当数据量达到该容量时，系统执行一次 full compaction，将全部数据合并到垂直部分。水平部分的结构十分灵活，可以根据 workload 的特征进行调整。具体而言，它有两个可配置的控制参数。(1) Merge Policy：可在 leveling merge policy 与 tiering merge policy 之间切换。(2) Level 数量 $\ell$：在 leveling policy 下，level 数量越少，读性能越好；在 tiering policy 下，level 数量越少，写性能越好。第 5.2 节将介绍 Vertiorizon 如何对这些参数进行自调优。

**垂直部分。** 垂直部分由 LSM-tree 中最大的两个 level 构成，每个 level 都像垂直方案一样具有固定容量。当水平部分达到总容量时，其全部数据通过 full compaction 合并到垂直部分的第一个 level。当第一个 level 被填满后，数据通过 partial compaction 移至第二个 level，也就是最大的 level。与水平部分类似，垂直部分也可以在 leveling 与 tiering merge policy 之间切换，并且包含一个可调参数，即垂直方案中相邻 level 之间的大小比 $T$。具体来说，默认情况下，垂直部分第一个 level 的容量是水平部分总大小的 $T$ 倍，即 $n \cdot T \cdot B$。第二个 level 的容量是第一个 level 的 $T$ 倍，即 $n \cdot T^2 \cdot B$。由于垂直部分两个 level 之间的 partial compaction 只会引起很小的空间放大和写停顿，与全程采用 full compaction 的 LSM-tree 相比，Vertiorizon 可以将空间放大和写停顿大致降低至原来的 $1/T$。

人们或许会问，为什么我们根据经验将垂直部分固定为两个 level。原因在于，垂直部分的主要目的是通过在最大的若干 level 之间启用 partial compaction，避免过度的空间放大和写停顿。通过让最大的两个 level 使用垂直方案，并在它们之间应用 partial compaction，与全程采用 full compaction 的 LSM-tree 相比，Vertiorizon 可以将空间放大和写停顿显著降低至大约原来的 $1/T$。我们发现，在实践中，这样的设置已经足够且有效。进一步增加垂直部分中的 level 数量，就必须按比例缩小水平部分的规模，从而削弱水平部分带来的收益。需要注意的是，即使垂直部分的 level 数量固定，我们仍可以通过调节参数 $T$ 在写放大和空间放大之间进行权衡。增大 $T$ 可以进一步降低整体空间放大和写停顿，但代价是垂直部分的写放大增加。

**运行示例。** 图 6 展示了 Vertiorizon 摄入新数据时的一个典型过程。起初，水平部分已经准备好开始增长，而垂直部分第一个 level 中的数据量接近其满容量。当水平部分被填满后，它开始向垂直部分的第一个 level 执行 compaction。该过程会清空所有水平 level。随后，当垂直部分第一个 level 中的数据量超过其容量时，数据开始通过 partial compaction 逐步移入最大的 level；之后，水平部分开始新一轮增长。

*图 6. Vertiorizon 的一般过程。*

**水平部分的动态调整。** 为了在数据量增长时维持 LSM-tree 的结构，Vertiorizon 会动态增大控制水平部分容量的参数 $n$。具体而言，$n$ 会依据数据规模初始化为一个适中的值。当垂直部分的最大 level 达到其容量时，在下一次 full compaction 清空水平部分之后，我们将 $n$ 增加 $1/T$ 倍，并相应调整垂直部分的容量。由于该调整发生在水平部分被完全清空之后，因此不会产生额外开销。通过这种方式，Vertiorizon 下 LSM-tree 的总体容量可以逐步扩展。

**通过调整大小比优化写放大。** 我们可以通过调整垂直部分中最大的两个 level 的大小比，进一步优化 Vertiorizon 的写放大。这两个 level 很特殊，因为这里发生了从采用 full compaction 的 level 到采用 partial compaction 的 level 的过渡，从而为优化总体写放大提供了机会。

由于垂直部分采用 partial compaction，其第一个 level 中的数据量始终保持在接近容量的位置。因此，从水平部分到垂直部分第一个 level 的 compaction 总会产生大小为 $T$ 的写放大因子，因为该 level 中的数据量维持在水平部分容量的 $T$ 倍。相比之下，从垂直部分第一个 level compact 到第二个 level 时产生的平均写放大仅为 $(T+1)/2$。出现这一降低，是因为此前的研究表明，partial compaction 会导致 level 内的数据密度不均匀 [35]。由于存在这些差异，将两个大小比都设为相同的 $T$，会使垂直部分两个 level 上的 compaction 产生不同的写放大，导致总体写放大并非最优。具体来说，我们将水平部分与垂直部分第一个 level 之间的大小比设为 $T'$，将垂直部分第一和第二个 level 之间的大小比设为 $T^2/T'$。该设置保证 Vertiorizon 中最大 level 的容量保持不变，从而维持 LSM-tree 的总体规模。在此设置下，垂直部分第一个 level 的写放大变为 $T'$，第二个 level 的写放大则调整为 $(T^2/T' + 1)/2$。注意

$$
T' + \frac{T^2/T' + 1}{2} \geq 2\sqrt{T' \cdot \frac{T^2}{2T'}} + \frac{1}{2} = \sqrt{2}T + \frac{1}{2}. \tag{2}
$$

上述不等式来自算术—几何平均不等式（AM-GM Inequality），当 $T' = T/\sqrt{2}$ 时等号成立。

sections.title: 5.2 Self-Tuning for Various Workloads
sections.titleTranslation: 5.2 面向不同 Workload 的自调优
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Cost Model for Horizontal Part.** Vertiorizon self-tunes its horizontal part to ensure it reaches the optimal point on the read-write trade-off curve for any given workload. To this end, we have carefully analyzed the cost of each operator and proposed a cost model for the horizontal part.

**Point and Range Lookup Cost.** A point lookup probes each sorted run from top to bottom. As the total data range is at least the size of the largest level in the vertical scheme, which is significantly larger than the total data size in the horizontal part, the probability of an arbitrary key appearing in the horizontal part is very low. Therefore, we model the lookup cost of the horizontal-leveling scheme by multiplying the number of levels $\ell$ with the false positive rate of the Bloom filters $f$ (i.e. $R_l = \ell \cdot f$). The analysis of the lookup cost for the horizontal-tiering scheme is quite intricate. Since each run exists for varying and irregular periods, they contribute differently to the overall read cost. Below we present a novel analysis results in the following lemma stating the lookup cost under horizontal-tiering scheme. Please refer to our technical report [1] for a complete proof.

**Lemma 5.1.** Let $m$ be the integer that satisfies $\binom{m}{\ell} \leq n \leq \binom{m+1}{\ell}$. Then the lookup cost of the horizontal-tiering scheme can be computed by the following equation.

$$
R_t = \left(\ell \cdot \binom{m}{\ell+1} + (m-\ell+1) \cdot \left(n-\binom{m}{\ell}\right)\right) \cdot \frac{f}{n}. \tag{3}
$$

In terms of incurred overhead, the primary distinction between range lookups and point lookups lies in their interaction with the runs in the horizontal part of the LSM-tree. Specifically, for each existing run, point lookups typically result in a disk I/O operation only in cases of false positives of Bloom filters, whereas range lookups invariably incur a disk I/O for every run. Aside from this difference, the derivations for point lookups and range lookups are identical. Therefore, the range lookup costs under leveling and tiering satisfy $Q_l = R_l/f$ and $Q_t = R_t/f$, respectively.

**Update Cost.** With tiering, each entry would only be involved in one compaction at each level. Therefore, the I/O cost per update under the horizontal-tiering scheme can be modeled by dividing the number of levels $\ell$ by the page size in entries $P$, reminding that each disk I/O moves an entire page of entries in bulk. Furthermore, we employ the following lemma to calculate the update cost of the horizontal-leveling scheme.

**Lemma 5.2.** Let $m$ be the integer that satisfies $\binom{m}{\ell} \leq n \leq \binom{m+1}{\ell}$. Then the update cost of the horizontal-leveling scheme can be represented as the following equation.

$$
W_l = \left(\ell \cdot \binom{m+1}{\ell+1} + (m+1) \cdot \left(n-\binom{m}{\ell}\right) - (\ell-1) \cdot n\right) \cdot \frac{1}{n \cdot P}. \tag{4}
$$

**Navigate Toward the Best Design.** For a given design, we can weight its I/O cost of the update, point lookup, and range lookup $W$, $R$, and $Q$ with their respective proportion in the workload, denoted as $w$, $r$, and $q$, to derive the average per operation I/O cost incurred by this design.

$$
\zeta = w \cdot W + r \cdot R + q \cdot Q. \tag{5}
$$

We explore all possible configurations for the horizontal part of Vertiorizon. Specifically, leveraging the convexity of the cost functions under both leveling and tiering merge policies with respect to the number of levels $\ell$, we initiate our search from the minimum feasible value, $\ell = 2$ and increase $\ell$. The optimal setting for each merge policy is identified by locating the saddle point of the cost function $\zeta$, where the function transitions from decreasing to increasing. Finally, we select the superior design between the leveling and tiering merge policies. To enhance the efficiency of self-tuning, we employ binary search, noting that the maximum value of $\ell$ cannot exceed $n$. Therefore, the complexity of the self-tuning process is only $O(\log_2 n)$, which is efficient practically.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**水平部分的成本模型。** Vertiorizon 对其水平部分进行自调优，以确保对于任意给定 workload，它都能到达读写权衡曲线上的最优点。为此，我们仔细分析了每种操作的成本，并为水平部分提出了一个成本模型。

**点查询与范围查询成本。** 点查询自上而下探测每个 sorted run。由于总数据范围至少与垂直方案中最大 level 的大小相同，而后者显著大于水平部分的总数据量，因此任意 key 出现在水平部分的概率很低。所以，我们用 level 数量 $\ell$ 乘以 Bloom filter 的假阳性率 $f$，对 horizontal-leveling 方案的查询成本建模（即 $R_l = \ell \cdot f$）。horizontal-tiering 方案的查询成本分析则相当复杂。由于各个 run 存在的时长不同且不规则，它们对总体读成本的贡献也不同。下面我们给出一个新的分析结果，并通过以下引理说明 horizontal-tiering 方案下的查询成本。完整证明请参阅我们的技术报告 [1]。

**引理 5.1。** 设 $m$ 是满足 $\binom{m}{\ell} \leq n \leq \binom{m+1}{\ell}$ 的整数，则 horizontal-tiering 方案的查询成本可由下式计算：

$$
R_t = \left(\ell \cdot \binom{m}{\ell+1} + (m-\ell+1) \cdot \left(n-\binom{m}{\ell}\right)\right) \cdot \frac{f}{n}. \tag{3}
$$

就所产生的开销而言，范围查询与点查询的主要区别在于它们和 LSM-tree 水平部分中各个 run 的交互。具体而言，对于每个现有 run，点查询通常只有在 Bloom filter 发生假阳性时才产生一次磁盘 I/O；而范围查询对每个 run 都必然产生一次磁盘 I/O。除这一差异外，点查询和范围查询的推导完全相同。因此，在 leveling 和 tiering 下，范围查询成本分别满足 $Q_l = R_l/f$ 和 $Q_t = R_t/f$。

**更新成本。** 在 tiering 下，每个数据项在每个 level 只会参与一次 compaction。因此，horizontal-tiering 方案下每次更新的 I/O 成本，可以用 level 数量 $\ell$ 除以以数据项数量计量的页大小 $P$ 来建模；这里要注意，每次磁盘 I/O 都会批量移动一整页的数据项。此外，我们使用以下引理计算 horizontal-leveling 方案的更新成本。

**引理 5.2。** 设 $m$ 是满足 $\binom{m}{\ell} \leq n \leq \binom{m+1}{\ell}$ 的整数，则 horizontal-leveling 方案的更新成本可表示为：

$$
W_l = \left(\ell \cdot \binom{m+1}{\ell+1} + (m+1) \cdot \left(n-\binom{m}{\ell}\right) - (\ell-1) \cdot n\right) \cdot \frac{1}{n \cdot P}. \tag{4}
$$

**趋向最佳设计。** 对于给定设计，我们可以按照更新、点查询和范围查询在 workload 中各自所占的比例 $w$、$r$ 和 $q$，对它们各自的 I/O 成本 $W$、$R$ 和 $Q$ 加权，从而得到该设计产生的平均单操作 I/O 成本。

$$
\zeta = w \cdot W + r \cdot R + q \cdot Q. \tag{5}
$$

我们探索 Vertiorizon 水平部分的所有可能配置。具体来说，利用 leveling 和 tiering merge policy 下成本函数关于 level 数量 $\ell$ 的凸性，我们从最小可行值 $\ell = 2$ 开始搜索并逐渐增大 $\ell$。对于每种 merge policy，通过定位成本函数 $\zeta$ 由下降转为上升的鞍点来确定其最优设置。最后，我们从 leveling 与 tiering merge policy 中选择更优的设计。为提高自调优效率，我们采用二分搜索，并注意到 $\ell$ 的最大值不会超过 $n$。因此，自调优过程的复杂度仅为 $O(\log_2 n)$，在实践中十分高效。

sections.title: 5.3 Optimization for Skewed Workloads
sections.titleTranslation: 5.3 针对倾斜 Workload 的优化
sections.originalMarkdown:
As an optional technique, we can further optimize our technique by making use of skewness information. We adopt the definition of skewed distribution from previous works [11], which introduces a sharp distinction between hot and cold keys for analytical convenience. The skewed distribution comprises two distinct uniform distributions: $U_h$ and $U_c$, where $U_h$ represents a small set of frequently updated hot keys, and $U_c$ denotes a much larger set of regular cold keys. We assume that the hot key set has a high probability of appearing in each buffer flush, and it is likely that each subsequent run containing all the keys from the hot set. For instance, under a uniform workload, merging two runs of size $D$ during compaction results in a new run of size $2D$ because the keys are unlikely to overlap. In contrast, under a skewed workload, both runs contain the hot keys, and obsolete versions are discarded during compaction. This leads to a run size of only $2D-U_h$, while $U_h$ redundant hot keys are eliminated. Therefore, under the leveling merge policy, each level grows more slowly with each compaction as the workload becomes more skewed. Whereas, under the tiering merge policy, the impact of workload skewness on performance is less significant because new data flowing in each level does not merge with existing data.

We then discuss how we adapt the compaction strategy when the impact of skewness is more pronounced, i.e., under the leveling merge policy. The write amplification of a level, incurred by the compaction of its upper level, is proportional to the current size of the level. Under skewed workloads, the growth of levels is slower than in a uniform workload because more duplicate entries appear in a compaction, leading to a slower increase in write amplification. Consequently, we see a diminished benefit of performing compaction, which can reduce the write amplification of subsequent compactions. Building on this insight, it is beneficial to align the compaction frequency with the workload skewness. Interestingly, only minor modifications to Algorithm 1 are necessary. Specifically, we introduce a coefficient $\alpha = U_h/B$ to quantify the degree of workload skewness, where $B$ is the buffer size. This coefficient also reflects the slowdown in the growth of the first level’s size under a skewed workload, as each hot key has a high probability of appearing in each buffer flush. As the workload becomes more skewed, or $\alpha$ increases, compactions should occur less frequently since the level grows slower. This adjustment is implemented by increasing the compaction trigger threshold of the first level based on skewness $\alpha$. While there are multiple ways to achieve the adjustment, in this paper, we change the condition in Line 6 in Algorithm 1 to $C_i > C_{i+1} + \delta$ when $i = 1$, where $\delta$ is the largest integer satisfying

$$
\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \geq \frac{\delta \cdot (\delta+1)}{2}. \tag{6}
$$

In Equation 6, the term $\alpha/(1-\alpha)$ represents a skewness-related coefficient, reflecting the ratio of hot keys in each flush. Notably, it shows an approximately quadratic relationship with $\delta$. Equation 6 is grounded in a deep intuition comparing the benefits and costs in adjusting $\delta$. Due to space limitation, we refer interested readers to our technical report [1] for a rigorous derivation of this equation.
sections.translationMarkdown:
