作为一种可选技术，我们可以利用倾斜度信息进一步优化所提出的方法。我们采用此前工作 [11] 对倾斜分布的定义。为了便于分析，该定义明确区分 hot key 与 cold key。倾斜分布由两个不同的均匀分布 $U_h$ 和 $U_c$ 组成，其中，$U_h$ 表示一小组被频繁更新的 hot key，$U_c$ 表示规模大得多的一组常规 cold key。我们假设 hot key 集合以很高的概率出现在每次 buffer flush 中，并且后续的每个 run 都很可能包含 hot key 集合中的所有 key。例如，在均匀 workload 下，由于 key 不太可能重叠，在 compaction 期间合并两个大小为 $D$ 的 run 会得到一个大小为 $2D$ 的新 run。相反，在倾斜 workload 下，两个 run 都包含 hot key，且过期版本会在 compaction 期间被丢弃。因此，所得 run 的大小仅为 $2D-U_h$，其中有 $U_h$ 个冗余 hot key 被消除。所以，在 leveling merge policy 下，workload 越倾斜，每个 level 在每次 compaction 后的增长就越慢。而在 tiering merge policy 下，由于流入每个 level 的新数据不会与已有数据合并，workload 倾斜度对性能的影响较小。

接下来，我们讨论当倾斜度影响更为显著时，也就是在 leveling merge policy 下，如何调整 compaction 策略。由上层 level 的 compaction 引起的某一 level 的写放大，与该 level 的当前大小成正比。在倾斜 workload 下，由于一次 compaction 中会出现更多重复数据项，level 的增长速度比均匀 workload 下更慢，因而写放大的增长也更慢。由此，执行 compaction 所带来的收益会减小；这里的收益是降低后续 compaction 的写放大。基于这一认识，让 compaction 频率与 workload 倾斜度相匹配是有益的。有意思的是，我们只需对算法 1 做很小的修改。具体而言，我们引入系数 $\alpha = U_h/B$ 来量化 workload 的倾斜程度，其中 $B$ 是 buffer 大小。由于每个 hot key 都以很高概率出现在每次 buffer flush 中，该系数也反映了倾斜 workload 下第一个 level 大小增长速度的减缓程度。workload 越倾斜，即 $\alpha$ 越大，level 的增长就越慢，compaction 也应越少发生。我们根据倾斜度 $\alpha$ 提高第一个 level 的 compaction 触发阈值来实现这一调整。实现这种调整的方法有多种；在本文中，当 $i = 1$ 时，我们将算法 1 第 6 行的条件改为 $C_i > C_{i+1} + \delta$，其中 $\delta$ 是满足下式的最大整数：

$$
\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} \geq \frac{\delta \cdot (\delta+1)}{2}. \tag{6}
$$

在公式 6 中，$\alpha/(1-\alpha)$ 是与倾斜度相关的系数，它反映每次 flush 中 hot key 的比例。值得注意的是，它与 $\delta$ 近似呈二次关系。公式 6 建立在比较调整 $\delta$ 的收益与成本这一深层直觉之上。由于篇幅限制，我们请感兴趣的读者参阅技术报告 [1]，其中给出了该公式的严格推导。

sections.title: 5.4 Vertiorizon as a Backbone
sections.titleTranslation: 5.4 以 Vertiorizon 作为骨干结构
sections.originalMarkdown:
For existing LSM-based designs using the vertical scheme, our improved Vertiorizon can serve as a backbone to replace the vertical scheme in enhancing their system performance.

For example, we consider the well-known LSM-based design lazy-leveling, proposed in Dostoevsky [15]. In lazy-leveling, the largest level of the LSM-tree is configured using the leveling merge policy, while the remaining levels employ tiering. We can embed Vertiorizon into this design by replacing all the tiering levels with the horizontal part of Vertiorizon utilizing the horizontal-tiering scheme. Specifically, suppose the original lazy-leveling design has $L$ levels. To maintain consistency during embedding, we set the number of levels $\ell$ in the horizontal part to $L-1$ and adjust its capacity to $B \cdot T^{L-1}$, corresponding to the size of the largest level being replaced. In this way, we integrate Vertiorizon into lazy-leveling without altering the original configuration. Since the number of levels using tiering remains unchanged after the replacement, the update cost is the same as that of the original lazy-leveling design. Meanwhile, the lookup cost is improved thanks to the nice property of horizontal scheme (Theorem 4.2).

**Introducing New Bloom Filter Layout.** Some LSM-based designs employ the Monkey Bloom filter layout [14] to improve their point lookup performance. The idea is to allocate higher bits-per-key for Bloom filters at smaller levels. However, the optimization strategy rests on the assumption that the data size within every level consistently remains at that level’s capacity. This assumption is not accurate under the full compaction granularity employed in many state-of-the-art LSM-based designs [18, 36, 55]. Because each full compaction empties a level after merging all its data. Consequently, the level would progress from being vacant to reaching its full capacity before another compaction occurs. This problem also impedes the compatibility of the Monkey layout with Vertiorizon.

To address this problem, we propose a new dynamic Bloom filter layout, which is designed to achieve better read optimization under the horizontal scheme by more precisely modeling full compaction processes. Unlike the static allocation of the Bloom filter under the Monkey layout, the dynamic layout adaptively adjusts the Bloom filter’s allocation in response to changes in data volume for each level and incorporates future changes in data volume into the model’s considerations. However, directly adjusting Bloom filter’s allocation requires fetching the original data indexed by the Bloom filter from disk into memory in order to reconstruct the Bloom filter based on the new bits per key, which incurs significant additional overhead. To circumvent this issue, we never directly adjust Bloom filter’s allocation under the dynamic layout. Each time a full compaction occurs between two levels and data is loaded into memory, we simultaneously reallocate and reconstruct the Bloom filter associated with those two levels.
sections.translationMarkdown:
对于现有的、采用垂直方案的 LSM-based 设计，我们改进后的 Vertiorizon 可以作为骨干结构来替换垂直方案，从而提升其系统性能。

例如，我们考虑 Dostoevsky [15] 提出的知名 LSM-based 设计 lazy-leveling。在 lazy-leveling 中，LSM-tree 最大的 level 采用 leveling merge policy，其余 level 则采用 tiering。我们可以把 Vertiorizon 嵌入该设计：用采用 horizontal-tiering 方案的 Vertiorizon 水平部分替换所有 tiering level。具体而言，假设原始 lazy-leveling 设计有 $L$ 个 level。为了在嵌入过程中保持一致，我们将水平部分的 level 数量 $\ell$ 设为 $L-1$，并将其容量调整为 $B \cdot T^{L-1}$，与被替换的最大 level 的大小相对应。这样，我们便可以在不改变原始配置的情况下，将 Vertiorizon 集成到 lazy-leveling 中。由于替换后采用 tiering 的 level 数量保持不变，其更新成本与原始 lazy-leveling 设计相同。同时，得益于水平方案的良好性质（定理 4.2），查询成本得到改善。

**引入新的 Bloom Filter 布局。** 一些 LSM-based 设计采用 Monkey Bloom filter 布局 [14] 来改善点查询性能。其思想是在较小的 level 上为 Bloom filter 分配更高的 bits-per-key。然而，该优化策略建立在一个假设之上：每个 level 内的数据量始终维持在该 level 的容量。对于许多先进 LSM-based 设计 [18, 36, 55] 所采用的 full compaction 粒度，这一假设并不准确。因为每次 full compaction 都会在合并一个 level 的全部数据后将其清空。因此，在另一次 compaction 发生前，该 level 会经历从空置到达到满容量的过程。这个问题也阻碍了 Monkey 布局与 Vertiorizon 的兼容。

为解决该问题，我们提出一种新的动态 Bloom filter 布局。它通过更精确地对 full compaction 过程建模，在水平方案下实现更好的读优化。不同于 Monkey 布局对 Bloom filter 的静态分配，动态布局会响应每个 level 数据量的变化，自适应地调整 Bloom filter 的分配，并在模型中考虑数据量未来的变化。然而，直接调整 Bloom filter 的分配，需要将 Bloom filter 所索引的原始数据从磁盘取入内存，以根据新的 bits-per-key 重建 Bloom filter，这会产生显著的额外开销。为避免这一问题，在动态布局下，我们从不直接调整 Bloom filter 的分配。每当两个 level 之间发生 full compaction、数据被载入内存时，我们同时重新分配并重建与这两个 level 相关联的 Bloom filter。

sections.title: 6 Additional Related Works
sections.originalMarkdown:
**LSM-tree-based Designs.** Recent LSM-tree-based optimizations include exploring innovative compaction policies and routines [11, 14–16, 18, 28, 29, 48, 50, 51], improving Bloom filters [14, 17, 62, 63] and range filters [32, 40, 60], as well as harnessing novel hardware for performance gains [3, 27, 54, 56, 59, 61]. Techniques have also been investigated for key-value separation [9, 37], managing in-memory hot entries [5], enhancing concurrency [24, 52], and prefix indexing [40, 57, 60]. Furthermore, some efforts have been dedicated to practical performance enhancements by reducing tail latency [6, 38, 51], exploiting workload characteristics [2, 49, 58], and refining the memory distribution [8, 31, 39]. To the best of our knowledge, most of these works are either based on the vertical growth scheme or are orthogonal to the growth scheme, as the vertical scheme is typically considered the default in LSM-trees.

**Studies on Horizontal Growth Scheme.** Over the past decade, several studies [41–45] have recognized the advantages of the horizontal scheme and have focused on its analysis and application. Mathieu et al. [44] analyzed and optimized the horizontal growth scheme in BigTable from the perspective of competitive ratios. In contrast, Mao et al. [41–43] conducted a series of experimental studies comparing the vertical and horizontal schemes under regular and spatial workloads. Although these studies are insightful, their efforts primarily concentrated on theoretical and experimental investigations of the existing horizontal growth scheme using the leveling merge policy, without proposing new designs. In this paper, we further enhance the applicability of the horizontal scheme through a novel design. Specifically, we introduce the horizontal-tiering scheme, which extends the horizontal scheme to the tiering merge policy, and present Vertiorizon, an enhanced growth scheme that embodies the strengths of both the vertical and horizontal schemes.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**LSM-tree-based 设计。** 近期的 LSM-tree-based 优化包括：探索创新的 compaction policy 与流程 [11, 14–16, 18, 28, 29, 48, 50, 51]，改进 Bloom filter [14, 17, 62, 63] 和 range filter [32, 40, 60]，以及利用新型硬件获得性能提升 [3, 27, 54, 56, 59, 61]。研究人员还探索了 key-value separation [9, 37]、内存 hot entry 管理 [5]、并发性增强 [24, 52] 和前缀索引 [40, 57, 60] 等技术。此外，也有一些工作致力于实践中的性能增强，包括降低尾延迟 [6, 38, 51]、利用 workload 特征 [2, 49, 58]，以及优化内存分配 [8, 31, 39]。据我们所知，由于垂直方案通常被视为 LSM-tree 的默认方案，这些工作大多要么基于垂直增长方案，要么与增长方案正交。

**关于水平增长方案的研究。** 在过去十年中，一些研究 [41–45] 已经认识到水平方案的优势，并专注于对其进行分析和应用。Mathieu 等人 [44] 从竞争比的角度分析并优化了 BigTable 中的水平增长方案。相比之下，Mao 等人 [41–43] 开展了一系列实验研究，在常规 workload 和空间 workload 下比较垂直方案与水平方案。尽管这些研究很有启发性，但它们主要集中于对采用 leveling merge policy 的现有水平增长方案进行理论与实验研究，并未提出新的设计。在本文中，我们通过一种新颖设计进一步增强水平方案的适用性。具体来说，我们提出 horizontal-tiering 方案，将水平方案扩展到 tiering merge policy；同时提出 Vertiorizon，这是一种融合垂直方案与水平方案各自优势的增强增长方案。

sections.title: 7 Evaluation
sections.originalMarkdown:
By default, our experiments are conducted on a server with Intel(R) Gold 6326 CPU @ 2.90GHz processor, 256GB DDR4 memory, and 1TB NVMe SSD, running 64-bit Ubuntu 20.04.4 LTS on an ext4 partition.

**Baselines and Implementation.** We assess Vertiorizon (abbr. VRN) against ten baselines. The implementation of Vertiorizon and all the baselines are built on Facebook’s RocksDB [20], a popular LSM-tree-based key-value storage system and also been employed by many previous LSM-tree studies [9, 14–16, 18, 37, 39, 49, 59, 60].

The first four baselines are all possible combinations of compaction schemes and merge policies discussed in our technical section: vertical-leveling (abbr. VT-Level-Part), vertical-tiering (abbr. VT-Tier-Part), horizontal-leveling (abbr. HR-Level), and horizontal-tiering (abbr. HR-Tier). By default, the vertical schemes employ partial compaction, while the horizontal schemes use full compaction. This configuration makes vertical-leveling equivalent to the default setup of RocksDB. For the other three baselines, we extended RocksDB’s code to implement them. We also included two additional baselines derived from the default RocksDB (i.e., Vertical-Level-Part): “Universal” and “RocksDB-Tuned”. They share most of the same settings with Vertical-Level-Part but are extended in two key ways. Specifically, Universal enables RocksDB’s universal compaction [22], an age-based compaction scheme aligned with the tiering merge policy, whereas RocksDB-Tuned applies additional tunings to certain RocksDB parameters specific to the vertical growth scheme.[^3] Furthermore, although not commonly used in practical applications, designs based on the vertical scheme can adopt full compaction to sacrifice fine-grained file-level compaction for improved performance. This leads to two additional baselines: vertical-leveling with full compaction (abbr. VT-Level-Full) and vertical-tiering with full compaction (abbr. VT-Tier-Full). Additionally, we compare two designs based on Vertiorizon but without self-tuning. Instead, they use a fixed leveling design and a fixed tiering design with $\ell = 2$ in the horizontal part, referred to as VRN-Level and VRN-Tier, respectively.

[^3]: Specifically, we enable "level_compaction_dynamic_level_bytes", set "max_bytes_for_level_multiplier" to 10, and configure "compaction_pri" to "kOldestSmallestSeqFirst".

**Experimental Setting.** For all methods, we retain most orthogonal parameters as RocksDB’s default settings, which are known to be developer-optimized for general SSD-based applications [21]. Furthermore, following previous works [15, 28], we enable direct I/O by setting the parameters “use_direct_io_for_flush_and_compaction” and “use_direct_reads” to true in RocksDB. We configure the memory buffer size to 2 MB by setting “write_buffer_size” and “target_file_size_base” to 2,097,152, and set the LSM-tree size ratio $T = 6$ by assigning “max_bytes_for_level_multiplier” to 6. We evaluate the impact of different Bloom filter settings, varying the bits-per-key from 4 to 20 (Figure 8), while using a default value of 5 in other experiments, as this aligns with the latest RocksDB default. We also analyze the impact of block cache size, varying from 16MB to 64GB (Figure 8 (e)), with a default of either 32MB or 64GB in other experiments. Specifically, Figure 7, Figure 8 (a) and (d), Figure 10 (b) and (c), and Figure 9 (a) use a 32MB block cache, while Figure 8 (b) and (c), Figure 10 (d) and (e), along with Figure 9 (b) use a 64GB block cache. These two configurations are chosen because a 32MB block cache aligns with the default setting of the latest RocksDB version, while a 64GB block cache represents scenarios with abundant memory resources. Evaluating both settings reveals different performance rankings among the baselines, providing a more comprehensive understanding of system performance. We evaluate each under various workloads with different lookup and update ratios; we initially bulk-load $10^7$ key-value entries of 1KB into the LSM-tree. Each entry is generated through the YCSB benchmark with either uniform or Zipfian distribution, comprising a 128-byte key and an 896-byte value. For testing, we generate and process a workload with 4,000,000 operations, each can be a lookup or an update, and report the overall average throughput and the worst-case throughput of processing the workload. To measure the worst-case throughput, we maintain a time window containing the most recent 100,000 processed operations and record the lowest throughput observed within this window, which is then reported as the worst-case throughput. To measure space amplification, we record the peak disk space occupied by RocksDB’s storage directory during runtime. By subtracting the data size from the total space used by the LSM-tree, and then dividing the difference by the size of unique entries, we derive the additional space amplification per entry.

*Fig. 7. Vertiorizon shows desirable performance across all scenarios and metrics. Table (d) presents the ranking of each method under various metrics. (For methods, "L" means leveling, "T" means tiering, "P" means partial, "F" means full, and "VRN" is Vertiorizon. For workloads, "RH" is read-heavy, "B" is balanced, "WH" is write-heavy, and "RS" is range scan.)*

**Vertiorizon achieves desirable performance across all scenarios and dimensions.** In Figure 7 (a) and (b), we compare Vertiorizon against all baselines in terms of average throughput and worst-case throughput across various YCSB workloads under both uniform and Zipfian distributions, with y-axis standardized to facilitate comparison. The workloads include balanced workload (50% updates and 50% point lookups), write-heavy workload (90% updates and 10% point lookups), read-heavy workload (10% updates and 90% point lookups), and range scan workload (25% range lookups and 75% updates). Overall, the results under the uniform and Zipfian distributions are similar. From Figure 7 we have the following discussions.

*Results on read-heavy workload.* (1) Leveling-based approaches consistently achieve better overall performance compared to tiering-based methods, because tiering-based methods tend to suffer from high read amplification, resulting in lower throughput in read-heavy scenarios. (2) Under the vertical scheme, the full compaction method (e.g., VT-Level-Full) delivers superior average throughput compared to its corresponding partial compaction method (e.g., VT-Level-Part). This is because partial compaction introduces additional write amplification. However, the worst-case throughput of VT-Level-Full is lower than VT-Level-Part, because full compaction leads to significant write stalls when merging large levels. We also note that the worst-case throughput is also influenced by overall performance. For instance, VT-Tier-Part has the lowest average throughput among all vertical baselines, even though it incurs minimal write stalls with partial compaction. (3) Baselines under the horizontal scheme demonstrate higher average throughput than their vertical scheme counterparts with the same merge policy, owing to the optimal read-write trade-off of the horizontal scheme. For example, the average throughput of HR-Level is notably better than VT-Level-Full. Nevertheless, because these horizontal methods rely on full compaction, they suffer from reduced worst-case throughput due to write stalls. (4) Vertiorizon excels in both average and worst-case throughput by employing a hybrid layout that delivers strong read and write performance while alleviating write stalls. This design enables Vertiorizon to achieve strong average throughput and also maintain the best worst-case throughput among all methods.

*Results on write-heavy workload.* (1) All tiering-based methods exhibit desired overall performance, with HR-Tier achieving the highest average throughput and VT-Tier-Partical achieving the best worst-case throughput. In contrast, all leveling-based methods struggle in this read-heavy scenario. (2) Similar to our observations under the read-heavy workload, both VT-Tier-Full and HR-Tier experience lower worst-case throughput compared to partial or hybrid designs like VT-Tier-Part and Vertiorizon, due to write stalls caused by full compaction. However, the relative decline in worst-case throughput is less pronounced than that observed among leveling-based methods under the read-heavy workload. This is because, under the tiering merge policy, compaction data is not merged with existing data in the next level, resulting in smaller compacted run sizes compared to leveling and thus reduced write stalls. (3) Although VT-Tier-Part achieves the best worst-case throughput among tiering-based methods, it suffers from significantly lower average throughput—only 60% of HR-Tier’s average throughput. This degradation is attributed to partial compaction under tiering, which introduces higher write amplification and increased read amplification, as runs at each level are not cleared after compaction and continue to accumulate. (4) Most leveling-based baselines perform poorly overall. Among these, HR-Level shows relatively better performance due to the superior read-write trade-off of the horizontal scheme. (5) Meanwhile, Vertiorizon achieves satisfactory performance in both average throughput and worst-case throughput on write-heavy workload, through self-tuning to a tiering-based design similar to VRN-Tier.

*Results on balanced workload.* Under the balanced workload, Vertiorizon achieves the highest average throughput by automatically tuning to a design that optimally suits balanced read-write demands. Simultaneously, Vertiorizon maintains satisfactory worst-case throughput. Most of the baselines exhibit markedly lower average throughput, except for HR-Full, which attains average throughput close to the best. This performance is attributed to the optimal read-write trade-off provided by the horizontal scheme and its settings being well-positioned on the trade-off curve for the balanced workload. VT-Level-Part and VT-Tier-Part achieve the best worst-case throughput due to their use of partial compaction. However, this also leads to their average throughput being relatively poor among the baselines. Meanwhile, the worst-case throughput of several baselines employing the tiering merge policy is unsatisfactory. Finally, although VRT-Level and VRT-Tier adopt the same hybrid layout as Vertiorizon, their fixed settings prioritize either read or write performance at the expense of the other, resulting in mediocre performance under the balanced workload.

*Fig. 8. The performance with varied bits per key and block cache size.*

*Results on range scan workload.* The performance trends are similar to those observed under the read-heavy workload, because a range lookup is sustaintially more costly than a point lookup. Notably, we observe a more substantial variation in worst-case throughput across methods, as the heavier updates in this workload further exacerbates performance variability caused by write stalls.

*Assessing advanced variations of RocksDB.* From Figures 7(a) and (b), Universal consistently underperforms under all settings due to its simplistic trigger condition for universal compactions, which limits algorithmic efficiency. RocksDB-tuned typically surpasses Vertical-Level-Part (the default RocksDB configuration), especially under read-heavy workloads. However, its average throughput still struggles in update-intensive scenarios, as it is essentially based on the vertical scheme and the leveling merge policy.

*Comparison of space amplification.* Figure 7 (c) presents the space amplification under the balanced workload with uniform distribution. Among all the baselines, the two full-compaction methods, VT-Level-Full and HR-Level, entail the highest space amplification. In contrast, VT-Tier-Full and HR-Tier, which also use full compaction but with the tiering policy, have a lower space amplification. This reduction occurs because, under the tiering merge policy, compactions to a level do not merge with existing data, which would temporarily duplicate during the merge sort process. Compared methods with full compaction, the methods with partial compaction often entail much lower space amplification, as exemplified by VT-Level-Part and VT-Tier. Meanwhile, Vertiorizon has a quite desirable space amplification, as it adopts the vertical scheme with partial compaction in the largest levels.

*Overall performance ranking.* Figure 7 (d) presents the ranking of each method across various performance metrics, including average and worst-case throughput across all uniform workloads, as well as space amplification. Overall, the results demonstrate that Vertiorizon consistently delivers strong performance across all metrics, whereas each baseline falls short in at least one dimension of performance.

**The impact of large block cache and Bloom filters.** Figure 8 presents the throughput of each approach with large Bloom filters (up to 20 bits per key) and block cache (up to 64GB). The workloads for (d) and (e) follow a balanced distribution, consisting of 50% updates and 50% point lookups, uniformly distributed. Aside from these varying parameters, all other settings follow the settings in Figure 7. To examine whether Vertiorizon maintains its superiority under large Bloom filters and block cache, we present the corresponding performance rankings in Table 3, aligned with each subfigure in Figure 8.

*Table 3. Rankings of each method across different metrics for each case in Fig. 8 (rounded to nearest tenth).*

| Metrics | Case | VT-Level-Part (LP) | VT-Level-Full (LF) | VT-Tier-Part (TP) | VT-Tier-Full (TF) | HR-Level (L) | HR-Tier (T) | Vertiorizon (VRN) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Average | (a) | 6.0 | 4.5 | 5.5 | 3.5 | 3.3 | 2.5 | 2.0 |
| Average | (b) | 6.0 | 4.0 | 6.3 | 4.0 | 3.5 | 1.8 | 1.8 |
| Average | (c) | 6.3 | 5.3 | 5.3 | 3.3 | 5.0 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| Average | (d) | 6.4 | 5.2 | 4.4 | 2.8 | 4.2 | 3.0 | 1.8 |
| Average | (e) | 5.7 | 4.4 | 6.9 | 4.4 | 2.1 | 2.7 | 2.1 |
| Worst Case | (a) | 2.8 | 6.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 6.3 | 4.3 | 1.8 |
| Worst Case | (b) | 2.5 | 6.3 | 2.8 | 5.5 | 5.0 | 3.5 | 2.3 |
| Worst Case | (c) | 2.3 | 6.5 | 3.0 | 4.0 | 6.5 | 3.5 | 1.5 |
| Worst Case | (d) | 3.8 | 7.0 | 1.2 | 4.6 | 6.0 | 3.4 | 2.2 |
| Worst Case | (e) | 1.0 | 6.4 | 2.1 | 5.0 | 6.6 | 4.0 | 2.9 |
| Avg. | — | 4.3 | 5.6 | 4.1 | 3.9 | 4.9 | 3.0 | 2.0 |

Several key observations emerge when comparing these rankings with Figure 7. First, Vertiorizon remains highly competitive across all rankings and consistently achieves the highest overall performance. While our approach may not always be the best for a specific workload, this overall ranking highlights its robustness across various scenarios. In contrast, every other method exhibits at least one scenario where its effectiveness diminishes. Second, when ample memory resources are available, all approaches are expected to achieve better performance, resulting in closer performance across them. As shown in Figure 8 (d), a higher bits-per-key setting reduces read costs, leading to increased throughput. Similarly, Figure 8 (e) shows a slight performance improvement when the block cache size is large. This improvement is primarily driven by a higher cache hit rate. However, since frequent compactions tend to invalidate the block cache, the benefit remains limited even with a larger cache size. Third, since large Bloom filters and block caches generally enhance point-lookup performance, methods that prioritize write optimization (e.g., tiering methods) tend to improve their rankings. Notably, HR-Tier now ranks second in average throughput, making it more comparable to Vertiorizon. However, it is important to note that HR-Tier is also a novel approach introduced in this paper.

*Fig. 9. Evaluation of Vertiorizon under very large data scale.*

**Performance under larger datasets.** In Figure 9, we compare our method with the baselines under significantly larger memory buffer size and data size, aiming to offer a comparative analysis that reflects scenarios of the big data era. As such large-scale experiments have high demand on disk space, we use an alternate server with a larger disk capacity, specifically featuring a 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-13900K CPU @ 4.0GHz processor, 128GB DDR4 main memory, and a 2TB NVMe SSD. In this experiment, most configuration settings align with precedent experiments in Figure 7. Except for that for all methods, the buffer size is increased to 64 MB, and the total preloaded data is increased to 500 GB, corresponding to 500,000,000 key-value entries. We then run a workload of 200,000,000 operations to evaluate each method’s performance. Because loading such a large amount of data is time-consuming, we select a relatively general workload setting to conduct this experiment: the read-write balanced workload (50% updates and 50% point lookups) with a uniform key distribution. We use metrics similar in Figure 7 (a), including average throughput and worst-case throughput during workload processing. Figure 9 (a) uses a moderate memory configuration with 32MB block cache and 10 bits per key Bloom filter, while (b) employs a larger memory configuration with 64GB block cache and 20 bits per key Bloom filter.

We observe that when the data scale becomes very large, Vertiorizon excels in average throughput. In particular, its average throughput surpasses that of baselines such as HR-Level and HR-Tier, which had previously performed well under smaller-scale settings. The underlying reason is that these baselines rely exclusively on full compaction, leading to drastically severe write stalls at very large data scales and causing their worst-case throughput to deteriorate significantly. In contrast, by employing a hybrid growth scheme, Vertiorizon maintains a satisfactory worst-case throughput, trailing behind methods that fully adopt partial compaction (e.g., VT-Level-Part and VT-Level-Full). Nevertheless, because partial compaction is overall less efficient, these methods post only mediocre results in terms of average throughput.

*Fig. 10. (a) compares the trade-off between vertical scheme and horizontal scheme. (b) ∼ (e) shows that Vertiorizon can be applied to existing designs to enhance their performance. ("L" means the lazy-leveling design and "E" means lazy-leveling embedded with Vertiorizon.)*

**From horizontal-leveling to horizontal-tiering: enhanced read-write trade-off curve.** In Figure 10 (a), we compare the read-write trade-off curves of the vertical and horizontal schemes. In the vertical scheme, the trade-off between read and write performance is controlled by adjusting the size ratio $T$ and the merge policy. The horizontal scheme’s trade-off, conversely, is regulated by varying the number of levels $\ell$ and the merge policy, and is enhanced by our horizontal-tiering algorithm under tiering merge, To evaluate these schemes comprehensively, we assess ten different designs under each compaction scheme with varying settings. Specifically, for the vertical scheme, we test both partial compaction and full compaction designs with adjacency ratios $T = 4, 6, 8, 10$ under both leveling and tiering merge policies. Similarly, for the horizontal scheme, we evaluate designs with the number of levels $\ell = 3, 4, 6$ under both merge policies. We evaluate them on a workload consisting of 20 million operations with equal amounts of updates and point lookups. For each design, we plot a point on Figure 10 (a) based on its per-lookup and per-update latency, where the horizontal axis represents the read cost and the vertical axis represents the write cost.[^4] By connecting all the points corresponding to each compaction scheme, we obtain the read-write trade-off curve for that scheme in practical LSM-tree implementations.

[^4]: The figure shows that all methods achieve relatively low latencies in both lookups and updates, on the order of hundreds of microseconds. This is primarily attributed to the inherent efficiency of the LSM-tree data structure and the high data transfer bandwidth of the SSDs used in our experimental setup. Notably, previous studies [15, 28] have also reported that, under similar experimental conditions, RocksDB demonstrates operation latencies within the hundred-microsecond range.

The result shows that our proposed horizontal-tiering scheme significantly extends the trade-off curve of the original horizontal scheme, thereby completely enveloping that of both vertical-partial and vertical-full designs. The enhanced horizontal scheme’s trade-off dominates the vertical scheme’s, with the horizontal scheme’s curve consistently closer to the origin, indicating lower overall costs. This aligns with our earlier theoretical analysis, as the compaction sequence under the horizontal scheme is always optimal. The trade-off curve presented for the horizontal scheme effectively represents the Pareto frontier for LSM-trees in terms of compaction schemes.

**Vertiorizon enhances existing designs as a fundamental infrastructure.** We embedded Vertiorizon into various lazy-leveling designs and compared their performance with the original configurations. Each lazy-leveling design employed different size ratios $T$, specifically scaling across the values 4, 6, 8, and 10. We evaluated these designs using a balanced workload comprising 4 million operations, with an equal split of 50% updates and 50% point lookups. Figure 10 (b) and (d) present the total latency for each lazy-leveling design, as well as the individual read and write latencies under 32MB and 64GB block cache configurations, respectively. In this figure, the upper portion of each bar represents the read latency, while the lower portion corresponds to the write latency. The results demonstrate that embedding Vertiorizon into any lazy-leveling design consistently improves performance. This enhancement is primarily reflected in the reduction of read latency. The improvement arises because our embedding replaces the original tiering levels with the horizontal part of Vertiorizon utilizing the horizontal-tiering scheme, all while maintaining the same number of levels. This approach effectively reduces the average read amplification during runtime without increasing the write amplification. Furthermore, we also tested lazy-leveling designs with adapted filter layout [14]. As shown in Figure 10 (c) and (e), the results indicate that after incorporating Bloom filter optimizations, embedding Vertiorizon as a backbone continues to enhance the performance of lazy-leveling designs.

默认情况下，我们在一台服务器上进行实验；该服务器配备 Intel(R) Gold 6326 CPU @ 2.90GHz 处理器、256GB DDR4 内存和 1TB NVMe SSD，并在 ext4 分区上运行 64 位 Ubuntu 20.04.4 LTS。

**Baseline 与实现。** 我们将 Vertiorizon（缩写为 VRN）与十种 baseline 进行比较。Vertiorizon 及所有 baseline 均基于 Facebook 的 RocksDB [20] 实现。RocksDB 是一种流行的基于 LSM-tree 的 key-value 存储系统，许多以往的 LSM-tree 研究也采用了它 [9, 14–16, 18, 37, 39, 49, 59, 60]。

前四种 baseline 是技术章节所讨论的 compaction 方案与 merge policy 的全部可能组合：vertical-leveling（缩写为 VT-Level-Part）、vertical-tiering（缩写为 VT-Tier-Part）、horizontal-leveling（缩写为 HR-Level）和 horizontal-tiering（缩写为 HR-Tier）。默认情况下，垂直方案采用 partial compaction，而水平方案采用 full compaction。这样的配置使 vertical-leveling 等价于 RocksDB 的默认设置。对于其余三种 baseline，我们通过扩展 RocksDB 代码实现了它们。我们还加入了两种由默认 RocksDB（即 Vertical-Level-Part）派生的 baseline：“Universal”和“RocksDB-Tuned”。它们的大部分设置与 Vertical-Level-Part 相同，但在两个关键方面进行了扩展。具体而言，Universal 启用 RocksDB 的 universal compaction [22]，这是一种与 tiering merge policy 相一致、基于年龄的 compaction 方案；RocksDB-Tuned 则对垂直增长方案特有的若干 RocksDB 参数作了额外调优。[^3] 此外，尽管实际应用中并不常用，基于垂直方案的设计仍可采用 full compaction，以牺牲细粒度的文件级 compaction 来改善性能。这产生了另外两种 baseline：采用 full compaction 的 vertical-leveling（缩写为 VT-Level-Full）和采用 full compaction 的 vertical-tiering（缩写为 VT-Tier-Full）。此外，我们还比较了两种基于 Vertiorizon 但不进行 self-tuning 的设计。它们分别在水平部分使用固定的 leveling 设计和固定的 tiering 设计，并令 $\ell = 2$，二者分别称为 VRN-Level 和 VRN-Tier。

[^3]: 具体而言，我们启用 "level_compaction_dynamic_level_bytes"，将 "max_bytes_for_level_multiplier" 设为 10，并把 "compaction_pri" 配置为 "kOldestSmallestSeqFirst"。

**实验设置。** 对所有方法，我们将大部分正交参数保留为 RocksDB 的默认设置；已知这些设置由开发者针对一般的基于 SSD 的应用进行了优化 [21]。此外，沿用以往工作 [15, 28] 的做法，我们在 RocksDB 中将参数 “use_direct_io_for_flush_and_compaction” 和 “use_direct_reads” 设为 true，以启用 direct I/O。通过将 “write_buffer_size” 和 “target_file_size_base” 设为 2,097,152，我们把内存 buffer 大小配置为 2 MB；通过将 “max_bytes_for_level_multiplier” 设为 6，我们把 LSM-tree 的大小比设为 $T = 6$。我们评估不同 Bloom filter 设置的影响，将 bits-per-key 从 4 变化到 20（图 8）；其他实验中的默认值为 5，因为这与最新版 RocksDB 的默认值一致。我们还分析 block cache 大小的影响，其范围从 16MB 到 64GB（图 8(e)）；其他实验的默认值为 32MB 或 64GB。具体而言，图 7、图 8(a) 和 (d)、图 10(b) 和 (c) 以及图 9(a) 使用 32MB block cache；图 8(b) 和 (c)、图 10(d) 和 (e) 以及图 9(b) 使用 64GB block cache。之所以选择这两种配置，是因为 32MB block cache 与最新版 RocksDB 的默认设置一致，而 64GB block cache 则代表内存资源充足的场景。对两种设置进行评估会揭示 baseline 之间不同的性能排名，从而更全面地理解系统性能。我们在 lookup 与 update 比例不同的各种 workload 下评估每种方法；首先向 LSM-tree 批量加载 $10^7$ 个大小为 1KB 的 key-value entry。每个 entry 都通过 YCSB benchmark 按 uniform 或 Zipfian 分布生成，由 128-byte key 和 896-byte value 组成。测试时，我们生成并处理一个包含 4,000,000 次操作的 workload，每次操作可以是一次 lookup 或 update，并报告处理该 workload 的总体平均吞吐量和最坏情况吞吐量。为测量最坏情况吞吐量，我们维护一个包含最近 100,000 次已处理操作的时间窗口，记录该窗口内观察到的最低吞吐量，并将其报告为最坏情况吞吐量。为测量空间放大，我们记录运行期间 RocksDB 存储目录占用的峰值磁盘空间。先从 LSM-tree 使用的总空间中减去数据大小，再将差值除以唯一 entry 的大小，即可得到每个 entry 的额外空间放大。

*图 7. Vertiorizon 在所有场景和指标下均展现出理想性能。表 (d) 给出了各方法在不同指标下的排名。（对于方法，“L”表示 leveling，“T”表示 tiering，“P”表示 partial，“F”表示 full，“VRN”表示 Vertiorizon。对于 workload，“RH”表示 read-heavy，“B”表示 balanced，“WH”表示 write-heavy，“RS”表示 range scan。）*

**Vertiorizon 在所有场景和维度上均实现了理想性能。** 在图 7(a) 和 (b) 中，我们在 uniform 与 Zipfian 两种分布下的各种 YCSB workload 上，从平均吞吐量和最坏情况吞吐量两方面将 Vertiorizon 与所有 baseline 进行比较；为便于比较，y 轴经过标准化。Workload 包括 balanced workload（50% update 和 50% point lookup）、write-heavy workload（90% update 和 10% point lookup）、read-heavy workload（10% update 和 90% point lookup）以及 range scan workload（25% range lookup 和 75% update）。总体而言，uniform 与 Zipfian 分布下的结果相似。基于图 7，我们作如下讨论。

*Read-heavy workload 的结果。* (1) 基于 leveling 的方法始终比基于 tiering 的方法取得更好的整体性能，因为基于 tiering 的方法往往会遭受较高的读放大，导致其在 read-heavy 场景下吞吐量较低。(2) 在垂直方案下，full compaction 方法（例如 VT-Level-Full）的平均吞吐量优于相应的 partial compaction 方法（例如 VT-Level-Part）。这是因为 partial compaction 会引入额外的写放大。然而，VT-Level-Full 的最坏情况吞吐量低于 VT-Level-Part，因为 merge 大层时，full compaction 会导致显著的写停顿。我们还注意到，最坏情况吞吐量也受整体性能影响。例如，尽管 VT-Tier-Part 借助 partial compaction 只产生极少写停顿，但它在所有垂直方案 baseline 中的平均吞吐量最低。(3) 得益于水平方案的最优读写权衡，采用水平方案的 baseline 的平均吞吐量高于使用相同 merge policy 的垂直方案对应方法。例如，HR-Level 的平均吞吐量明显优于 VT-Level-Full。不过，由于这些水平方法依赖 full compaction，它们会因写停顿而遭遇较低的最坏情况吞吐量。(4) Vertiorizon 采用一种混合布局，在提供强劲读写性能的同时缓解写停顿，因此在平均吞吐量与最坏情况吞吐量两方面都表现出色。该设计使 Vertiorizon 不仅能获得强劲的平均吞吐量，还能在所有方法中保持最佳的最坏情况吞吐量。

*Write-heavy workload 的结果。* (1) 所有基于 tiering 的方法都展现出理想的整体性能，其中 HR-Tier 获得最高的平均吞吐量，VT-Tier-Partical 获得最佳的最坏情况吞吐量。相比之下，所有基于 leveling 的方法在这个 read-heavy 场景下表现不佳。(2) 与 read-heavy workload 下的观察类似，由于 full compaction 引发写停顿，VT-Tier-Full 和 HR-Tier 的最坏情况吞吐量都低于 VT-Tier-Part 和 Vertiorizon 等 partial 或混合设计。不过，与 read-heavy workload 下基于 leveling 的方法相比，此处最坏情况吞吐量的相对下降幅度较小。这是因为在 tiering merge policy 下，compaction 数据不会与下一层已有数据 merge，所以 compact 后的 run 小于 leveling 下的 run，写停顿也随之减少。(3) 尽管 VT-Tier-Part 在基于 tiering 的方法中取得了最佳的最坏情况吞吐量，但其平均吞吐量显著较低——只有 HR-Tier 平均吞吐量的 60%。这种下降源于 tiering 下的 partial compaction：它会引入更高的写放大和读放大，因为每层中的 run 在 compaction 后并未清空，而是会继续累积。(4) 大多数基于 leveling 的 baseline 整体表现不佳。其中，由于水平方案具有更优的读写权衡，HR-Level 的表现相对更好。(5) 与此同时，通过 self-tuning 到类似 VRN-Tier 的 tiering-based 设计，Vertiorizon 在 write-heavy workload 上的平均吞吐量和最坏情况吞吐量都取得了令人满意的表现。

*Balanced workload 的结果。* 在 balanced workload 下，Vertiorizon 会自动调优到最适合均衡读写需求的设计，从而取得最高的平均吞吐量。同时，Vertiorizon 还能保持令人满意的最坏情况吞吐量。除 HR-Full 的平均吞吐量接近最佳值之外，大多数 baseline 的平均吞吐量都明显较低。HR-Full 的这种表现源于水平方案提供的最优读写权衡，同时其设置恰好处于适合 balanced workload 的权衡曲线位置。VT-Level-Part 和 VT-Tier-Part 因采用 partial compaction 而取得最佳的最坏情况吞吐量，但这也导致它们的平均吞吐量在 baseline 中相对较差。与此同时，采用 tiering merge policy 的若干 baseline 的最坏情况吞吐量并不理想。最后，尽管 VRT-Level 和 VRT-Tier 采用了与 Vertiorizon 相同的混合布局，但其固定设置会优先照顾读性能或写性能，并以牺牲另一方为代价，导致它们在 balanced workload 下的性能平平。

*图 8. bits per key 和 block cache 大小不同时的性能。*

*Range scan workload 的结果。* 性能趋势与 read-heavy workload 中观察到的趋势相似，因为一次 range lookup 的成本远高于一次 point lookup。值得注意的是，不同方法的最坏情况吞吐量呈现出更显著的变化，因为该 workload 中较重的 update 进一步加剧了写停顿造成的性能波动。

*评估 RocksDB 的高级变体。* 从图 7(a) 和 (b) 可以看到，Universal 在所有设置下始终表现不佳，这是因为 universal compaction 的触发条件过于简单，限制了算法效率。RocksDB-tuned 通常优于 Vertical-Level-Part（RocksDB 默认配置），尤其是在 read-heavy workload 下。然而，它在 update-intensive 场景下的平均吞吐量仍然不佳，因为它本质上仍基于垂直方案和 leveling merge policy。

*空间放大比较。* 图 7(c) 给出了 uniform 分布的 balanced workload 下的空间放大。在所有 baseline 中，VT-Level-Full 和 HR-Level 这两种 full-compaction 方法产生的空间放大最高。相比之下，同样采用 full compaction 但使用 tiering policy 的 VT-Tier-Full 和 HR-Tier 的空间放大更低。这是因为在 tiering merge policy 下，对某层执行 compaction 时不会与该层已有数据 merge；后者会在 merge sort 期间形成临时副本。与采用 full compaction 的方法相比，采用 partial compaction 的方法通常产生低得多的空间放大，VT-Level-Part 和 VT-Tier 就是典型例子。与此同时，Vertiorizon 的空间放大十分理想，因为它在最大的几个层中采用了带 partial compaction 的垂直方案。

*整体性能排名。* 图 7(d) 给出了各方法在多种性能指标上的排名，包括所有 uniform workload 下的平均吞吐量、最坏情况吞吐量以及空间放大。总体而言，结果表明 Vertiorizon 在所有指标上始终表现强劲，而每种 baseline 都至少在一个性能维度上存在不足。

**大型 block cache 和 Bloom filter 的影响。** 图 8 给出了各方法在大型 Bloom filter（最高 20 bits per key）和 block cache（最高 64GB）下的吞吐量。(d) 和 (e) 中的 workload 采用 balanced 分布，由均匀分布的 50% update 和 50% point lookup 构成。除这些变化的参数外，其他所有设置均沿用图 7 的设置。为了检验 Vertiorizon 在大型 Bloom filter 和 block cache 下是否仍保持优势，我们在表 3 中给出了与图 8 各子图对应的性能排名。

*表 3. 图 8 各种情况下不同方法在各项指标上的排名（四舍五入至最接近的十分位）。*

| 指标 | 情况 | VT-Level-Part (LP) | VT-Level-Full (LF) | VT-Tier-Part (TP) | VT-Tier-Full (TF) | HR-Level (L) | HR-Tier (T) | Vertiorizon (VRN) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 平均值 | (a) | 6.0 | 4.5 | 5.5 | 3.5 | 3.3 | 2.5 | 2.0 |
| 平均值 | (b) | 6.0 | 4.0 | 6.3 | 4.0 | 3.5 | 1.8 | 1.8 |
| 平均值 | (c) | 6.3 | 5.3 | 5.3 | 3.3 | 5.0 | 1.5 | 1.5 |
| 平均值 | (d) | 6.4 | 5.2 | 4.4 | 2.8 | 4.2 | 3.0 | 1.8 |
| 平均值 | (e) | 5.7 | 4.4 | 6.9 | 4.4 | 2.1 | 2.7 | 2.1 |
| 最坏情况 | (a) | 2.8 | 6.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 6.3 | 4.3 | 1.8 |
| 最坏情况 | (b) | 2.5 | 6.3 | 2.8 | 5.5 | 5.0 | 3.5 | 2.3 |
| 最坏情况 | (c) | 2.3 | 6.5 | 3.0 | 4.0 | 6.5 | 3.5 | 1.5 |
| 最坏情况 | (d) | 3.8 | 7.0 | 1.2 | 4.6 | 6.0 | 3.4 | 2.2 |
| 最坏情况 | (e) | 1.0 | 6.4 | 2.1 | 5.0 | 6.6 | 4.0 | 2.9 |
| 平均排名 | — | 4.3 | 5.6 | 4.1 | 3.9 | 4.9 | 3.0 | 2.0 |

将这些排名与图 7 比较，可以得到几个关键观察。第一，Vertiorizon 在所有排名中仍极具竞争力，并始终取得最高的整体性能。尽管我们的方法未必在某一种特定 workload 上总是最佳，但这一总体排名突显出它在不同场景下的稳健性。相比之下，其他每种方法都至少存在一个效果减弱的场景。第二，当内存资源充足时，所有方法的性能都有望得到改善，使它们之间的性能更为接近。如图 8(d) 所示，较高的 bits-per-key 设置会降低读成本，从而提高吞吐量。类似地，图 8(e) 表明 block cache 较大时，性能会略有改善。这种改善主要由更高的 cache hit rate 驱动。然而，由于频繁 compaction 往往会使 block cache 失效，即使 block cache 更大，其收益仍然有限。第三，由于大型 Bloom filter 和 block cache 通常会改善 point-lookup 性能，优先优化写入的方法（例如 tiering 方法）的排名往往会提升。值得注意的是，HR-Tier 现在在平均吞吐量方面排名第二，因而更接近 Vertiorizon。不过，需要指出的是，HR-Tier 也是本文提出的一种新方法。

*图 9. 在超大数据规模下对 Vertiorizon 的评估。*

**更大数据集下的性能。** 在图 9 中，我们在显著增大内存 buffer 和数据规模的情况下比较本文方法与 baseline，旨在提供能够反映大数据时代场景的比较分析。由于此类大规模实验对磁盘空间要求很高，我们使用了另一台磁盘容量更大的服务器，具体配置为 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-13900K CPU @ 4.0GHz 处理器、128GB DDR4 主存和 2TB NVMe SSD。在该实验中，大部分配置与图 7 的先前实验一致。不同之处在于，对所有方法均将 buffer 大小提高到 64 MB，并将预加载数据总量增加到 500 GB，对应 500,000,000 个 key-value entry。随后，我们运行包含 200,000,000 次操作的 workload 来评估每种方法的性能。由于加载如此大量的数据非常耗时，我们选择了一个相对通用的 workload 设置来开展该实验：采用 uniform key 分布的读写 balanced workload（50% update 和 50% point lookup）。我们使用与图 7(a) 类似的指标，包括 workload 处理期间的平均吞吐量和最坏情况吞吐量。图 9(a) 采用中等内存配置，即 32MB block cache 和 10 bits per key Bloom filter；图 9(b) 则采用更大的内存配置，即 64GB block cache 和 20 bits per key Bloom filter。

我们观察到，当数据规模变得非常大时，Vertiorizon 在平均吞吐量方面表现出色。尤其是，它的平均吞吐量超过了 HR-Level 和 HR-Tier 等 baseline，而这些 baseline 此前在较小规模设置下表现良好。根本原因在于，这些 baseline 完全依赖 full compaction，在数据规模极大时会导致极其严重的写停顿，使其最坏情况吞吐量显著恶化。相比之下，Vertiorizon 采用混合增长方案，能够保持令人满意的最坏情况吞吐量，仅落后于完全采用 partial compaction 的方法（例如 VT-Level-Part 和 VT-Level-Full）。尽管如此，由于 partial compaction 的整体效率较低，这些方法在平均吞吐量方面仅取得中等结果。

*图 10. (a) 比较垂直方案与水平方案之间的权衡。(b) ∼ (e) 表明 Vertiorizon 可以应用于现有设计以增强其性能。（“L”表示 lazy-leveling 设计，“E”表示嵌入 Vertiorizon 的 lazy-leveling。）*

**从 horizontal-leveling 到 horizontal-tiering：增强的读写权衡曲线。** 在图 10(a) 中，我们比较垂直方案与水平方案的读写权衡曲线。在垂直方案中，通过调整大小比 $T$ 和 merge policy 来控制读写性能之间的权衡。相反，水平方案的权衡由改变层数 $\ell$ 和 merge policy 来调节，并由我们在 tiering merge 下的 horizontal-tiering 算法加以增强。为了全面评估这些方案，我们在每种 compaction 方案下评估十种设置各异的设计。具体来说，对于垂直方案，我们在 leveling 和 tiering 两种 merge policy 下，测试相邻层比值为 $T = 4, 6, 8, 10$ 的 partial compaction 与 full compaction 设计。类似地，对于水平方案，我们在两种 merge policy 下评估层数为 $\ell = 3, 4, 6$ 的设计。评估采用一个包含 2000 万次操作的 workload，其中 update 与 point lookup 数量相等。对于每种设计，我们根据其单次 lookup 和单次 update 延迟在图 10(a) 中绘制一个点；横轴表示读成本，纵轴表示写成本。[^4] 将每种 compaction 方案所对应的所有点连接起来，就得到该方案在实际 LSM-tree 实现中的读写权衡曲线。

[^4]: 图中显示，所有方法的 lookup 与 update 延迟都相对较低，处于数百微秒的量级。这主要归因于 LSM-tree 数据结构本身的高效率，以及实验设置所用 SSD 的高数据传输带宽。值得注意的是，以往研究 [15, 28] 也曾报告，在类似实验条件下，RocksDB 的操作延迟处于数百微秒范围内。

结果表明，我们提出的 horizontal-tiering 方案显著扩展了原始水平方案的权衡曲线，从而完全包围 vertical-partial 和 vertical-full 两类设计的权衡曲线。增强后的水平方案在权衡方面优于垂直方案：水平方案的曲线始终更接近原点，表明其总体成本更低。这与我们前面的理论分析一致，因为水平方案下的 compaction 序列始终是最优的。就 compaction 方案而言，这里给出的水平方案权衡曲线实际上代表了 LSM-tree 的 Pareto frontier。

**Vertiorizon 作为基础设施增强现有设计。** 我们将 Vertiorizon 嵌入多种 lazy-leveling 设计，并将其性能与原始配置进行比较。每种 lazy-leveling 设计采用不同的大小比 $T$，具体取值依次为 4、6、8 和 10。我们使用包含 400 万次操作的 balanced workload 评估这些设计，其中 50% 为 update，50% 为 point lookup。图 10(b) 和 (d) 分别给出了 32MB 和 64GB block cache 配置下，各种 lazy-leveling 设计的总延迟以及单独的读写延迟。在图中，每个柱子的上半部分表示读延迟，下半部分表示写延迟。结果表明，在任意 lazy-leveling 设计中嵌入 Vertiorizon 都能持续改善性能。这种增强主要体现为读延迟下降。之所以取得这种改善，是因为我们的嵌入方式在维持相同层数的同时，用采用 horizontal-tiering 方案的 Vertiorizon 水平部分替换了原有 tiering 层。这种方法在不增加写放大的情况下，有效降低了运行期间的平均读放大。此外，我们还测试了采用 adapted filter layout [14] 的 lazy-leveling 设计。如图 10(c) 和 (e) 所示，结果表明，加入 Bloom filter 优化后，以 Vertiorizon 作为骨干仍然能够增强 lazy-leveling 设计的性能。

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.originalMarkdown: In this paper, we revisit two established growth schemes for LSM-trees: the horizontal scheme and the vertical scheme. Through a comprehensive analysis of their respective strengths and limitations, we revoke the recognition of the horizontal scheme and present an enhanced horizontal scheme by extending its applicability to accommodate the tiering merge policy. Building on this foundation, we propose Vertiorizon, a self-designing growth scheme that leverages the individual strengths of both horizontal and vertical schemes. We demonstrate the practical benefits of the enhanced horizontal scheme and Vertiorizon by embedding these schemes into RocksDB and conducting extensive experiments. Notably, Vertiorizon achieves robust performance across a wide range of scenarios and metrics. These results highlight the potential of Vertiorizon to serve as a foundational infrastructure for improving general LSM-tree-based systems.
sections.translationMarkdown: 本文重新审视了两种成熟的 LSM-tree 增长方案：水平方案和垂直方案。通过全面分析二者各自的优势与局限，我们重新唤起了对水平方案的认识，并将其适用范围扩展到 tiering merge policy，从而提出一种增强的水平方案。在此基础上，我们提出 Vertiorizon，这是一种 self-designing 增长方案，能够利用水平方案与垂直方案各自的优势。我们将增强后的水平方案和 Vertiorizon 嵌入 RocksDB 并开展广泛实验，由此证明了它们的实际收益。值得注意的是，Vertiorizon 在广泛的场景和指标下都实现了稳健性能。这些结果突显出 Vertiorizon 作为基础设施改善一般基于 LSM-tree 的系统的潜力。
